Field Manual
Electrical Assessments, SPOF Analysis & Recommendations
for MV/LV Process Plants

Medium-Voltage / Low-Voltage Industrial Plant — Indicative ~2 MW Demand

Standard: IEC Voltage: 11 kV MV / 400–415 V LV Frequency: 50 Hz Demand: ~2 MW / ~2.35 MVA
Indicative / Training Material. All values, equipment ratings, and configurations in this manual are indicative only and are provided for training and familiarisation purposes. They do not constitute a design, specification, or safety document. All numeric values must be confirmed by detailed engineering studies before use in any real installation. Follow all applicable national and IEC standards, site safety rules, and employer procedures at all times.

About This Manual

This field manual is a self-paced reference for a junior electrical engineer assigned to assess the main electrical equipment of a medium-voltage / low-voltage (MV/LV) industrial process plant with an indicative demand of approximately 2 MW. It covers equipment identification, core electrical calculations, single-point-of-failure (SPOF) analysis, and practical recommendations.

How to Use This Manual

Work through the parts in order. Each part builds on the previous: start by identifying the equipment and understanding its function (Part 1), apply the key calculations (Part 2), then conduct the SPOF analysis (Part 3) and formulate recommendations (Part 4).

Use the sidebar to navigate between sections. Checklists in each part track your progress — tick items as you complete them; your progress is saved automatically in the browser. Print the manual (use the Print button) for field use; the sidebar is hidden and each section starts on a new page.

This manual is a single offline HTML file. It requires no internet connection and no installed software beyond a modern web browser. Checklist state is stored in the browser's local storage — use the same browser session to preserve progress.

Scope and Plant Configuration

The indicative plant configuration used throughout this manual is:

Supply11 kV utility incomer, metered at MV level
MV switchgear11 kV ring-main unit (RMU) with protection relay
Transformers2 × 1600 kVA, Dyn11, 11 kV / 415 V (TX-1 and TX-2)
LV main switchboardSplit-bus, 415 V, with normally-open bus-tie (LV-MSB)
Motor controlMultiple MCCs fed from LV-MSB sections
Standby generationDiesel generator (DG) with automatic transfer switch (ATS)
Total demand~2 MW (indicative); apparent power ~2.35 MVA at pf 0.85

Refer to the master single-line diagram (SLD) throughout for equipment locations and tag references.

The Assignment

Where you are in the workflow: before anything else, understand what the campaign is and what you are being asked to deliver.

The SPOF Assessment Campaign

You are one of two Electrical Engineers on a four-month campaign to deliver an Electrical Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Assessment across 21 water and desalination plants in the Central & Eastern sector (Riyadh, Madinah, Qassim and the Eastern Region). The core team is:

Role Count Primary responsibility
Project Manager (Electrical) 1 Programme, quality, HSE, client interface; reviews all deliverables before submission.
Electrical Engineer 2 Per-plant SPOF assessment, plant calculation packages, concept modifications, assessment forms. That is you.
Electrical Technician Optional Non-intrusive field surveys and asset inventory support only — not part of the core team.

What you are asked to do per plant

For each of the 21 plants you are expected to work autonomously through the following:

  • Desk study: review the existing single-line diagrams, equipment schedules and prior studies.
  • Plant calculation package: produce load-flow, short-circuit, protection coordination, and earthing & lightning assessments (with power-system software). Arc-flash (IEEE 1584) and RAM/RBD availability analysis are advantageous.
  • SPOF assessment: inventory and classify electrical single points of failure; assess criticality using failure-mode and effects analysis (FMECA per IEC 60812).
  • SLD verification: red-line the single-line diagrams against the as-found plant during site visits.
  • Concept modifications: for each recommended change, prepare a marked-up SLD, equipment outline specification, scheme description, sequence of operation and high-level bill of materials. Concept level only — not detailed design, procurement or construction.
  • Assessment forms: complete the four per-plant deliverable forms (see Section — The Four Deliverable Forms).
Note — indicative figures: All ratings, capacities and figures in this manual are indicative and tentative. They illustrate method and typical practice. Confirm every value against the plant's actual load list, short-circuit study, utility connection agreement and governing specifications before acting on them.

Site logistics — Central & Eastern sector

The 21 plants are distributed across four hubs. The map below shows a schematic national overview combined with a zoomed sector view. Geometry is not to scale; the per-hub split is indicative.

Key takeaway: You are an autonomous assessment engineer. Your job is to identify electrical single points of failure, quantify their risk, and recommend practicable mitigations — plant by plant, across 21 sites in four months.

The Four Deliverable Forms

Where you are in the workflow: understand the exact deliverables you must produce for every plant before you step on site.

For each of the 21 plants, the primary deliverables are four completed assessment forms plus the plant calculation package and any concept modification packages. The forms are described below. Blank templates are in Appendix F — Blank Forms.

Form What it contains When completed Who owns it
1. Document Inventory List of all electrical documents obtained or requested: single-line diagrams (revision, date, verified), equipment schedules, protection setting records, prior studies, maintenance records, drawings. Records what was available, what was missing, and what was verified on site. Started in desk study; updated on site visit; finalised after site. Lead Engineer (you) — PM reviews.
2. FMECA Form Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis (IEC 60812). One row per electrical item of significance: failure mode, local effect, system effect, existing controls, severity (S), occurrence (O), detectability (D), RPN or criticality number, and recommended action. Identifies and ranks the SPOFs. Populated during analysis phase after desk study and site visit; updated iteratively. Lead Engineer — PM quality-gates before submission.
3. Recommendations Register Ranked list of recommended modifications or improvements. One row per recommendation: reference number, description, priority (high/medium/low), estimated scope, applicable standard, link to FMECA row, and status (open / in progress / closed). The basis for the concept modification packages. Drafted after FMECA is complete; updated as concept modifications are developed. Lead Engineer — PM approves priority ranking before issue.
4. Site-Visit Report Factual record of the site visit: date, attendees, areas accessed, equipment observed, as-found conditions (nameplate data, breaker/switch status, visible condition concerns, any thermographic hot-spots if permitted), discrepancies against the SLD, and photographs index. Non-intrusive only — see the warning below. Completed on the day of the site visit; reviewed by PM before issue. Lead Engineer (with optional technician support for data capture).
Tip — form discipline: Open all four forms as soon as a plant is assigned. Fill the Document Inventory and Site-Visit Report header during desk study so the template is ready on site. Do not wait until after the visit to create the form.
Tip — traceability: Every row in the FMECA form should carry a unique reference that you can cite in the Recommendations Register. The PM uses this linkage when quality-checking your work.
Key takeaway: Four forms, completed in sequence — Document Inventory, FMECA, Recommendations Register, Site-Visit Report — are the non-negotiable deliverables for every plant. Start them early; update them iteratively.

Assessment Workflow at a Glance

Where you are in the workflow: map the full sequence so you can plan your time plant by plant.

Stage What you do Form(s) updated
1 — Pre-site desk study Request and review all available documents: SLDs (confirm revision / date), equipment schedules, prior calculations, protection setting records. Identify missing documents to chase on site. Prepare calculation model shell. Mark up a working copy of the SLD with questions. Brief the optional technician if deployed. Document Inventory (columns: document received, revision, status)
2 — Site visit (non-intrusive) Verify SLD against as-found installation; red-line discrepancies. Capture nameplate data (ratings, make/model, serial numbers) for all main equipment. Record breaker/switch configuration (normally open / closed). Note visible condition, housekeeping and reliability concerns. Photograph (where permitted) and index photos. Collect any documents not previously received. Strictly observe host permit-to-work and QHSE rules. Site-Visit Report (as-found data);
Document Inventory (documents obtained on site)
3 — Analysis (office) Update the calculation model with as-found data. Run load-flow, short-circuit, protection coordination and earthing/lightning studies. Identify and classify SPOFs from the verified SLD. Populate FMECA rows: failure mode, effects, controls, criticality (S × O × D). Rank items by criticality / risk. FMECA form (all columns)
4 — Recommendations For each high-criticality FMECA item, develop a concept modification: marked-up SLD, equipment outline specification, scheme description / sequence of operation, and high-level bill of materials. Re-run affected calculations on the modified arrangement. Populate the Recommendations Register with priority, scope and FMECA cross-reference. Recommendations Register (all columns)
5 — Report & issue PM reviews and quality-gates all four forms and the calculation package. Finalise the Site-Visit Report. Assemble the per-plant deliverable pack (forms + calculation package + concept modification packages + red-lined SLDs). Submit to PM for consolidation into the campaign report. All four forms finalised; calculation package issued.
Tip — iterate freely: The workflow above is a logical sequence, not a strict one-pass process. You will often loop back: a site finding may invalidate a desk-study assumption and require you to update the FMECA and re-run a calculation. Return to earlier stages whenever new information warrants it. The PM expects updated forms up until the submission milestone.
Key takeaway: Five stages — desk study, site visit, analysis, recommendations, report. The forms are living documents throughout; complete and finalise them in order.

How to Use This Manual

Where you are in the workflow: orient yourself to the manual before reading the technical content.

Manual structure — Parts 0 to 5 and Appendices

This manual is a single self-contained HTML file designed for field use (offline, printable). It is organised into six parts and a set of appendices:

Part Title What it gives you
Part 0 Orientation (this part) Campaign context, deliverable forms, assessment workflow, conventions.
Part 1 Foundations How to read a MV/LV SLD; every equipment tag and its function; power-flow walk-through; equipment schedule and applicable IEC standards.
Part 2 Calculations All core electrical calculations — load, transformer sizing, full-load current, short-circuit, voltage drop, PFC, cable selection, protection coordination, arc-flash, earthing — with worked examples and a formula card.
Part 3 SPOF Analysis SPOF identification method, a symbol library of failure points, FMECA and RBD techniques, reliability metrics, and a printable site checklist.
Part 4 Control Philosophy & Modifications DCS/SCADA/PMS integration, best-practice modification templates, grid-instability mitigations, modification concept packages.
Part 5 Field Tasks Step-by-step site procedures: desk study, site-visit checklist, drawing verification, analysis workflow, report assembly, and the first-assignment walk-through.
Appendices A – G Glossary, standards list, formula card, full tag table, blank forms, hardening guidance, MTBF reference data.

How to use the manual on a real assignment

  1. First time through: read Parts 0 and 1 in full to internalise the workflow and the tag scheme. Skim Part 2 to confirm you are comfortable with the calculation methods.
  2. Starting a new plant: open Part 5 — Desk Study Procedure and follow the checklist. Open the four blank forms from Appendix F and fill the header information.
  3. On site: use the Visual Inspection Checklist and the Drawing Verification Checklist in Part 5. The SPOF symbol library in Part 3 helps you spot failure points in real switchrooms.
  4. During analysis: use Part 2 for calculations and Part 3 for the FMECA method. Cross-reference to the Formula Card and MTBF Reference Data as needed.
  5. New to this type of assignment? Start with the first-assignment walk-through in Part 5 — it takes you step-by-step through one complete plant cycle.
Tip — sidebar navigation: Use the sidebar (left panel) to jump between sections. Checkboxes in checklist sections persist across sessions in the same browser — use them to track progress per plant.
Key takeaway: Parts 0 and 1 are the foundation — read them first. Parts 2–4 are your reference library. Part 5 is your task guide on every assignment. Use the first-assignment walk-through in Part 5 if you are new to this type of work.

Conventions Used in This Manual

Where you are in the workflow: agree the ground rules before diving into the technical content.

IEC basis, voltages and frequency

This manual follows IEC conventions throughout:

Parameter Value used in this manual Note
Standards basis IEC (primary); IEEE referenced where relevant (e.g. IEEE 1584, IEEE 493) ANSI device numbers (52, 87T, etc.) are used in SLD breaker notation as industry practice.
MV supply voltage 11 kV (indicative) Actual utility voltage must be confirmed per plant; could be 6.6 kV, 13.8 kV or other.
LV distribution voltage 400 / 415 V, 3-phase IEC utilisation voltage. Confirm per plant.
Frequency 50 Hz Confirm per plant.
MV earthing Resistance-earthed (NER) Typical for industrial MV; confirm per plant.
LV earthing TN-S (solid) Confirm per plant.

Equipment tag scheme

Every piece of equipment is identified by a tag. Use only the tags in the table below; do not invent new tags without updating the project reference document. The full tag table with functions is in Part 1 — Equipment Function Table and Appendix D — Tag Table.

Tag Equipment Voltage level
MV-METUtility revenue metering cubicle11 kV (MV)
MV-SWGRMV metal-clad switchgear11 kV (MV)
MV-NERNeutral Earthing Resistor11 kV (MV)
TX-1 / TX-2MV/LV power transformers (2 × 1600 kVA, indicative)11 kV / 400 V boundary
LV-MSBMain LV switchboard / PCC (split bus A/B)400 V (LV)
BTLV bus-tie circuit breaker (normally open)400 V (LV)
MCC-1 … MCC-nMotor Control Centres400 V (LV)
VFDVariable Frequency Drive(s)400 V (LV)
PFCPower factor correction (detuned capacitor bank)400 V (LV)
DGDiesel generator set400 V (LV)
ATSAutomatic Transfer Switch400 V (LV)
UPSUninterruptible Power Supply400 V AC / battery
DCDBDC battery and charger system (110 V DC)DC (MV switchgear supply)
DB / EDBDistribution board / Essential distribution board400 V (LV)

The "indicative figures" rule

Every numerical value in this manual — voltages, currents, kVA ratings, cable sizes, fault levels, reliability figures — is indicative and tentative. The values are sized to a representative ~2 MW industrial plant to illustrate calculation method and typical equipment selection. They are not a substitute for:

  • the plant's actual load list and demand study,
  • a site-specific short-circuit and load-flow study,
  • the utility connection agreement and network data,
  • the governing project specification and local regulations.

Always confirm figures against actual plant data before acting on them.

Warning — NON-INTRUSIVE surveys only. All site surveys under this campaign are non-intrusive. You must not open energised panels, operate switches or breakers, perform live electrical tests, or take measurements that require direct contact with live conductors. Visual inspection, nameplate reading, photograph capture and accessible meter readings are permitted. Strictly observe the host permit-to-work procedure, lock-out/tag-out rules and QHSE requirements (ISO 9001 / 14001 / 45001) at every plant. The NDA and applicable national cybersecurity data-handling requirements apply to all data collected on site.
Key takeaway: IEC basis, 11 kV / 400–415 V / 50 Hz, the tag scheme above, all figures indicative — and every site survey is non-intrusive. These four rules underpin everything in this manual. Part 1 now walks you through the standard MV/LV plant architecture you will encounter on most of these 21 sites.

The MV/LV Plant at a Glance

Where you are in the workflow: before you can assess a plant, you must be able to read its single-line diagram and name every item on it. Part 1 builds that foundation.

Design basis of the reference plant

Throughout this manual, calculations and examples are illustrated using a representative ~2 MW industrial process plant (water / desalination service, typical of the sites you will assess). The design basis is summarised below; all figures are indicative and tentative.

Parameter Indicative value Note
Total plant maximum demand ~2.0 MW Confirm against actual load list.
Overall power factor (before correction) 0.85 lagging Indicative; varies with motor mix and VFD loading.
Apparent power (uncorrected) ~2.35 MVA S = P / pf = 2000 / 0.85
Installed transformer capacity (with margin) 2 × 1600 kVA +20–25 % design/diversity margin; next standard size above 2 × ~1175 kVA.
Utility incoming voltage (MV) 11 kV, 3-phase, 50 Hz Actual utility voltage confirmed per plant; could differ.
Distribution / utilisation voltage (LV) 400 / 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz IEC standard; confirm per plant.
MV system earthing Resistance-earthed via NER Limits earth-fault current to ~300–400 A (indicative).
LV system earthing TN-S (solid) Confirm per plant.
Redundancy philosophy N-1 partial — 2 × transformers + N.O. bus-tie; single DG for essential loads One transformer can carry the essential load if the other is lost.

Worked example — Transformer sizing from demand

Given ~2 MW demand at 0.85 power factor, size the transformer bank:

S = P / pf = 2000 kW / 0.85 ≈ 2353 kVA

Apply a design and diversity margin of +20–25 %:

S_design = 2353 × 1.25 ≈ 2941 kVA

Split across two transformers (N-1 split-bus), select next standard size:

S_each = 2941 / 2 ≈ 1471 kVA → select 2 × 1600 kVA

Result: 2 × 1600 kVA, Dyn11, 11 kV / 0.4 kV (tags TX-1 and TX-2). Values are indicative.

Power architecture — the master SLD

The master single-line diagram below shows the complete reference power architecture. Study it before proceeding — every section of Part 1 refers to items on this drawing.

The architecture has three tiers:

  1. MV (11 kV) tier: Utility feed → metering (MV-MET) → MV switchgear (MV-SWGR) with protection IEDs (MV-PROT) and neutral earthing resistor (MV-NER).
  2. Transformation tier: Two independent transformer feeders supply TX-1 and TX-2, each stepping 11 kV down to 400 V.
  3. LV (400 V) tier: Main switchboard (LV-MSB) with a split bus (A and B) joined by a normally-open bus-tie (BT); Motor Control Centres (MCC-1, MCC-2), PFC bank, distribution boards, essential board (EDB), diesel generator (DG), ATS, UPS and DC battery system (DCDB).
Key takeaway: The ~2 MW reference plant is the template for everything in this manual. Two 1600 kVA transformers on a split LV bus with a normally-open bus-tie give partial N-1 resilience — understanding why this architecture is used, and where it is still vulnerable, is the core of the SPOF assessment.

How to Read an SLD

Where you are in the workflow: before the site visit, you must be comfortable reading any MV/LV SLD you are handed, not just the reference drawing.

Power-flow direction and voltage levels

Read an SLD top to bottom — energy flows from the utility source at the top to the loads (motors, boards) at the bottom. On the reference drawing there are two clearly separated horizontal zones:

  • MV zone (upper band): 11 kV — from the utility intake down to and including the transformer primaries. MV busbars are drawn in firebrick red on colour drawings.
  • LV zone (lower band): 400 V — from the transformer secondaries to all distribution equipment and loads. LV busbars are drawn in navy blue.

The two transformers (TX-1, TX-2) are the physical and electrical boundary between the MV and LV zones.

IEC 60617 symbols used on the reference SLD

Note — IEC 60617: This is the international standard defining graphical symbols for diagrams. The symbols used on the reference SLD are drawn in accordance with IEC 60617 series. If you encounter a drawing using ANSI/IEEE symbols (common in North America), the layout is the same but some symbols differ — the most common example is the circuit-breaker symbol (IEC: a solid square; ANSI: two diverging lines). Always check the drawing legend before interpreting unfamiliar symbols.
Symbol / notation Meaning Where seen on reference SLD
Heavy horizontal line (═══) Busbar — the common copper to which feeders and incomers connect. 11 kV bus in MV-SWGR; LV Bus A and Bus B in LV-MSB.
Solid square on conductor Circuit breaker (closed / in service). IEC 60617-07. All MV and LV breakers.
Hollow / dashed square Circuit breaker normally open (N.O.). Bus-tie BT; ATS open position.
[52-I], [52-T1], etc. MV circuit breaker — ANSI device number 52. Suffix identifies the function (I = incomer, BS = bus-section, T1/T2 = transformer feeder). MV-SWGR breakers.
[ACB] Air Circuit Breaker — large LV breaker used for main incomers and bus-tie. LV-MSB incomers from TX-1 and TX-2; bus-tie BT.
[MCCB] Moulded-Case Circuit Breaker — smaller LV breaker used for outgoing feeders. LV-MSB outgoing feeders to MCC-1, MCC-2, PFC, DB, EDB.
Two interlinked circles with a line between ((│ │) with 8) Two-winding transformer (IEC 60617). The winding configuration label (e.g. Dyn11) shows delta primary (D), star secondary (y, solidly earthed), and 30° phase shift (clock position 11). TX-1 and TX-2.
Resistor symbol to earth at neutral point ([N]) Neutral Earthing Resistor — MV neutral earthed through a resistor. MV-NER at the 11 kV neutral.
Three diminishing bars to earth Earth / ground connection. LV transformer secondary neutral; earthing points on equipment.
Circle with CT or VT Current Transformer / Voltage Transformer — instrument transformers feeding meters and relays. MV-MET (revenue CTs/VTs); MV-PROT (protection CTs).
Circle with M Motor load. Process motors at the bottom of each MCC feeder.
Two parallel plates Capacitor bank (PFC). PFC on LV Bus A feeder.
Labelled rectangle (block) Equipment assembly — used for MCC, VFD, UPS, ATS, DB, DCDB where a single-symbol representation is appropriate. MCC-1, MCC-2, VFD, UPS, ATS, DCDB, DB, EDB.
DOL / S/S / VFD label on motor feeder Starter type: Direct-on-Line / Soft-Starter / Variable Frequency Drive. Within MCC blocks.

Busbar convention and the split bus

The LV main switchboard (LV-MSB) is drawn as a split bus: Bus A (left, fed by TX-1) and Bus B (right, fed by TX-2) are two physically separate copper bars in the one board. They are joined only through the normally-open bus-tie (BT). In normal operation each transformer supplies its own half of the plant independently. The split-bus arrangement:

  • limits the fault current that a single transformer must interrupt,
  • contains the blast zone of any bus fault to one section, and
  • allows the bus-tie to close automatically on loss of one incomer, maintaining supply to both buses from the surviving transformer.

Red-lining on site

On a site visit, you compare the SLD against the as-found installation and mark discrepancies in red on a working print or a PDF annotation tool. Common discrepancies to look for:

  • Breaker labelling or device number differs from drawing.
  • An extra or missing feeder — a circuit on site not shown on the drawing.
  • Bus-tie in a different normal position (open or closed) from the drawing.
  • Equipment substituted (e.g. RMU instead of metal-clad switchgear).
  • Protection relay type or settings different from what is listed.
  • Cables or transformers replaced with a different rating.
Key takeaway: Read SLDs top-to-bottom (source to load), know the IEC 60617 symbols, and understand the split-bus concept. On site, every discrepancy between drawing and reality is a red-line — and potentially a SPOF waiting to be found.

PCC vs MCC

Where you are in the workflow: this distinction is fundamental — confusing the two is one of the most common errors on assessment forms.

Both the PCC and the MCC are LV assemblies built to IEC 61439-1/-2, and they can look similar in a switchroom. They do very different jobs:

Feature PCC (Power Control Centre) MCC (Motor Control Centre)
Tag in reference plant LV-MSB MCC-1, MCC-2
Primary function Receives transformer output(s); distributes bulk LV power to downstream boards and feeders. The LV power hub. Houses individual motor starters — DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD — that control and protect specific process motors.
Incomers Air Circuit Breakers (ACBs) from transformers; motorised bus-tie ACB. Single MCCB incomer from the PCC (LV-MSB) outgoing feeder.
Outgoing feeders MCCBs feeding MCCs, distribution boards, PFC bank, large individual loads. Per-motor buckets: MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactors, overload relay, local/remote control, ammeter, run/trip indication.
Bus rating (indicative) ~2500–3200 A; 50–65 kA fault withstand. ~1000–2000 A; fault withstand matched to upstream board.
Form of segregation (IEC 61439) Form 4b — busbars separated from functional units; terminals separated from other functional units. Form 3b/4b — functional units (starters) separated from each other.
Where on the SLD The central LV switchboard spanning Bus A and Bus B — immediately below the transformers. On outgoing MCCB feeders from the LV-MSB Bus A/B; one MCC per bus section in the reference plant.
One-line description "The LV power hub — it feeds the MCCs." "Where the motors are started — it feeds the loads."
Note — vocabulary variation: In different organisations, the PCC is also called the Main LV Switchboard (MLVSB), Main Distribution Board (MDB), or simply the Main Board. The MCC may be called a Motor Control Panel (MCP) or a starter board. The function — bulk distribution vs. individual motor control — is the reliable distinguishing criterion, regardless of what the nameplate says.
Tip — SPOF implication: Because the PCC is the single point through which all transformer power passes before reaching the MCCs, a fault or loss of the PCC is a plant-level SPOF. The split bus with bus-tie is specifically designed to limit this vulnerability. When assessing a plant, check whether the PCC is truly split-bus or is effectively a single-bus board with cosmetic segregation.
Key takeaway: PCC (LV-MSB) = bulk distribution hub, fed by transformers, feeds MCCs. MCC = motor starter assembly, fed by PCC, feeds individual motors. The PCC feeds the MCCs; the MCCs feed the loads.

Equipment Function Table

Where you are in the workflow: this is the core reference table for the assessment — you will return to it every time you encounter a tag on site or in a document.

The table below covers all 17 tags in the reference plant (including MV-PROT). For each tag it states the equipment name, its function (what it does and why it is there), the indicative rating, and where it appears on the master SLD. All ratings are indicative; confirm against actual plant nameplates.

Tag Equipment Function / purpose Indicative rating Where on the SLD
MV-MET Utility revenue metering cubicle Measures energy and maximum demand at the 11 kV intake via utility-grade CTs and VTs feeding a tariff kWh/kVAh meter. The commercial boundary between utility and plant — the utility reads this meter for billing. Utility CT/VT and tariff meter; class 0.2S or better. MV section, first item on the 11 kV supply side (top of drawing).
MV-SWGR 11 kV metal-clad switchgear Receives and distributes the 11 kV supply. Houses the MV incomer breaker [52-I], bus-section breaker [52-BS], and two transformer-feeder breakers [52-T1] / [52-T2], so that MV circuits can be switched and fault current cleared quickly. The 11 kV busbar inside this board is the MV power hub. 11 kV, 630–1250 A bus, 25 kA / 1 s; vacuum CBs; IEC 62271-200. MV section, central board below metering.
MV-PROT MV protection and control IEDs Detects MV faults and trips the correct breaker to isolate them quickly, minimising damage and restoring supply elsewhere. Functions: overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), transformer differential (87T), under/over-voltage (27/59). Relays are supplied from the DCDB so they remain operative even on AC failure. Numerical IEDs; one per breaker or combined; IEC 60255 series. MV section, relay panels associated with MV-SWGR breakers.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor Earths the 11 kV transformer star neutral through a resistor that limits the earth-fault current to a safe, detectable level (~300–400 A indicative). Without it, an undetected earth fault could cause equipment damage and personnel hazard. A neutral CT allows the protection relay to detect and measure the fault current. Resistance sized for ~300–400 A earth-fault level (indicative); with neutral CT. MV section, at the [N] neutral point — connected to the neutral of the 11 kV supply transformer or to the MV bus neutral where provided.
TX-1 MV/LV power transformer No. 1 Steps 11 kV down to 400 V to supply Bus A of the LV-MSB. One of the two parallel sources that give the plant its partial N-1 resilience. If TX-2 is lost, the bus-tie closes so TX-1 can carry both buses. 1600 kVA, 11 kV / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %, IEC 60076; cast-resin or oil-immersed. Between MV feeder [52-T1] and LV incomer [ACB-A] on Bus A.
TX-2 MV/LV power transformer No. 2 Steps 11 kV down to 400 V to supply Bus B of the LV-MSB. Mirrors TX-1 on the Bus B side. If TX-1 is lost, the bus-tie closes so TX-2 can carry both buses. 1600 kVA, 11 kV / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %, IEC 60076. Between MV feeder [52-T2] and LV incomer [ACB-B] on Bus B.
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC (Power Control Centre) The 400 V power hub: receives the two transformer outputs on a split bus (A and B) and distributes bulk LV power via MCCB feeders to the MCCs, PFC bank, distribution boards and large individual feeders. ACB incomers from both transformers; motorised normally-open bus-tie (BT) between the two bus sections. 400 V, split bus A/B, ~2500–3200 A bus, 50–65 kA; ACB incomers + motorised bus-tie; Form 4b; IEC 61439-1/-2. LV section — the main board spanning both Bus A and Bus B, directly below the transformers.
BT LV bus-tie circuit breaker (normally open) Normally open during operation so the two bus sections are independent. Automatically closes on loss of either LV incomer (trip of ACB-A or ACB-B), allowing the surviving transformer to supply both buses. This is the single most important N-1 feature in the LV distribution — its correct operation (auto-close logic armed, correct setting) must always be verified. ACB, motorised/automatic; matching LV-MSB rating. LV-MSB — the link between Bus A and Bus B (drawn with hollow/N.O. symbol).
MCC-1 Motor Control Centre No. 1 (Bus A) Houses the motor starters (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter and/or VFD buckets) that control and protect the Bus-A process motors. Each bucket contains: MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactor(s), thermal/electronic overload relay or motor protection relay, local/remote control switch, run/trip indication and ammeter. 400 V; bus rating matched to group motor demand; IEC 61439-1/-2; Form 3b/4b. LV-MSB Bus A, on an outgoing MCCB feeder.
MCC-2 Motor Control Centre No. 2 (Bus B) Mirrors MCC-1 on Bus B — houses the starters for Bus-B process motors. Distributing motors across two bus sections means the plant retains some motor availability even if one transformer section is lost. 400 V; bus rating matched to group motor demand; IEC 61439-1/-2. LV-MSB Bus B, on an outgoing MCCB feeder.
VFD Variable Frequency Drive(s) Provides continuously variable speed control of pumps, fans, compressors and mixers by varying output voltage and frequency. Reduces energy consumption and mechanical starting stress compared with DOL starting. May be installed as individual MCC buckets or as standalone panels. Input chokes and EMC filters required to limit harmonic injection into the LV bus. Rated per driven motor; input choke and EMC filter; IEC 61800 series. Within MCC-1 / MCC-2 buckets, or as standalone panels; feeding speed-controlled motors.
PFC Power factor correction (detuned capacitor bank) Supplies reactive power locally to correct the plant power factor from ~0.85 to 0.95+ lagging, reducing demand charges and freeing transformer and cable capacity. Automatic switching (contactor-controlled steps) matches reactive output to load variation. Detuned (7 % tuning reactor in series) to prevent resonance with VFD-generated harmonics. Automatic; detuned (7 % reactor); sized to correct ~0.85 → 0.95+; IEC 60831. LV-MSB Bus A, on a dedicated MCCB feeder.
DB Distribution board(s) Distributes LV power to general (non-essential) loads — lighting, small power, HVAC, sockets — via MCBs and RCBOs. Fed from the LV-MSB via MCCB feeders. These loads are not critical: they are shed on a utility outage before or when the generator takes over the essential bus. 400/230 V; MCB/RCBO outgoing ways; IEC 60439 / IEC 61439. LV-MSB Bus A (and/or Bus B), on MCCB outgoing feeders.
EDB Essential / emergency distribution board Feeds loads that must remain energised through a utility outage — escape lighting, fire systems, critical control auxiliaries, communication systems. Supplied from the LV-MSB Bus B via an MCCB feeder in normal operation, and backed by the ATS so that on utility loss it is transferred to the generator within the ATS changeover time. 400/230 V; MCB/RCBO outgoing; IEC 61439. LV section — fed from Bus B (normal) and backed via ATS from DG.
DG Diesel generator set Standby generation sized to carry the essential load on utility failure — not necessarily the full 2 MW plant demand. Starts automatically (auto-start logic) on detection of utility loss; the ATS transfers the essential bus once the generator has reached rated voltage and frequency. Equipped with AVR, fuel day-tank, acoustic canopy and exhaust system. ~800–1250 kVA indicative (size confirmed by essential-load list); 400 V, 50 Hz, AVR. Standby power path (lower band of drawing).
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch Automatically transfers the essential bus between the utility feed (normal source) and the generator (standby source) on utility loss, and retransfers when utility supply is restored and stable. Mechanically interlocked to prevent both sources feeding the bus simultaneously — an important safety feature. Changeover time is the key performance parameter (typically 10–30 s for diesel start + transfer). Rated for essential bus load; mechanically interlocked; IEC 60947-6-1. Standby path — between DG output and EDB incoming supply.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply Provides clean, uninterrupted AC power to the DCS, PLC, SCADA, instrumentation and critical communications with no break — not even the ~20 s it takes the diesel generator to start and stabilise. Operates from internal batteries during the outage bridge; static bypass allows maintenance without interrupting the load. Battery autonomy is typically 15–30 min (confirm per spec). Rated per DCS/instrument load; static bypass; battery 15–30 min (indicative); IEC 62040. LV-MSB Bus B feeder, dedicated UPS output to DCS/control system.
DCDB DC battery and charger system (110 V DC) Provides secure 110 V DC power to trip and close the MV switchgear breakers, and to power the protection relay IEDs and their communications. This DC supply must remain available even when the entire AC system (both utility and generator) is dead — battery float charging maintains the battery at full charge continuously, and the battery alone sustains protection and switching capability during a complete AC blackout. 110 V DC (typical); battery sized per IEC 62485-2; float/boost charger; IEC 60896. DC control/protection supply band — feeds MV-SWGR trip/close circuits and MV-PROT IEDs.
Note — tag MV-PROT: The protection IEDs are sometimes shown as part of the MV-SWGR cubicle rather than as a separate tag, depending on the drawing convention. On site, check whether the relay panel has its own tag plate. For FMECA purposes, MV-PROT is treated as a distinct item because its failure mode (relay malfunction or loss of DC supply) is independent of the switchgear mechanism failure.
Key takeaway: Seventeen tags, two voltage levels, three functional layers — generation of the supply (MV), distribution (LV-MSB), and control/protection (DCDB/UPS/DG/ATS). Memorise the function of each tag in one sentence; you will need that knowledge to populate the FMECA without hesitation.

Power-Flow Walk-Through

Where you are in the workflow: tracing the three power paths is what you do mentally at every site visit — it reveals where the architecture is robust and where it is not.

Keep the master SLD (Fig 1-1) open alongside this section. There are three distinct paths through the plant — trace each one on the drawing as you read.

Path 1 — Main (normal) path: utility to process motor

  1. Utility 11 kV feed arrives at the site and passes through the revenue metering cubicle (MV-MET) where energy is metered for tariff billing.
  2. The supply enters the MV switchgear (MV-SWGR) via incomer breaker [52-I]. The protection IEDs (MV-PROT) watch this incomer and the transformer feeders for faults. The 11 kV neutral is earthed through the resistor (MV-NER).
  3. Two transformer feeder breakers [52-T1] and [52-T2] supply the two transformers (TX-1 and TX-2), which step 11 kV down to 400 V.
  4. Each transformer secondary feeds its LV incomer ACB: TX-1 → ACB-A → Bus A, TX-2 → ACB-B → Bus B. The bus-tie (BT) between them is normally open, so the two bus sections are independent.
  5. Outgoing MCCB feeders from Bus A and Bus B supply the Motor Control Centres (MCC-1, MCC-2), the PFC bank (PFC), distribution boards (DB) and the essential board (EDB).
  6. Inside an MCC, the appropriate starter (DOL, soft-starter or a VFD) energises the process motor — pump, fan, conveyor or mixer.
Tip — tracing on a real SLD: On a site SLD that may be larger or more complex, follow the same top-to-bottom discipline and identify: (a) the metering point, (b) the MV incomer and switchgear, (c) the transformer(s), (d) the LV main board and its bus arrangement, (e) the MCC feeders, and (f) the standby and DC paths. This sequence works on any MV/LV plant drawing.

Path 2 — Standby path: on utility loss

When the utility supply is lost (or falls outside acceptable voltage/frequency limits):

  1. The ATS detects utility loss and issues an auto-start signal to the diesel generator (DG).
  2. The DG starts, ramps to rated speed, and the AVR brings the voltage to 400 V ± tolerance.
  3. The ATS transfers the essential bus from the utility feed to the DG output — the transfer is mechanically interlocked to ensure only one source feeds the EDB at a time.
  4. The essential distribution board (EDB) remains energised — escape lighting, fire systems and critical process auxiliaries stay live.
  5. Within the LV-MSB, if one incomer ACB trips (loss of one transformer), the bus-tie (BT) auto-closes so the surviving transformer can supply both Bus A and Bus B (at reduced capacity — load shedding may be needed).
Note — ATS changeover time: A diesel generator takes typically 10–30 s to start, synchronise and accept load (indicative). During this window, only the UPS-backed loads remain energised. The EDB is de-energised for this interval unless the ATS uses a static (electronic) transfer or the EDB has its own small UPS. Verify the actual changeover time on site and confirm it is acceptable for the essential loads.

Path 3 — DC / control path: always alive

The control and protection path is designed to be available at all times — even during a complete AC blackout:

  1. The DCDB (DC battery and charger) is float-charged from the LV supply and holds its battery at full charge continuously. On loss of AC, the battery sustains the DC bus.
  2. The 110 V DC bus from the DCDB feeds the trip and close coils of the MV switchgear breakers and the MV protection relay IEDs. The protection system remains operative — it can still detect faults and trip breakers — even with no AC available.
  3. The UPS is float-charged from the LV AC bus (typically Bus B). On loss of AC it switches seamlessly to its own battery (static changeover, no break). It supplies the DCS, PLC, SCADA and instrumentation at clean regulated AC.
  4. Together, the DCDB and UPS ensure that both protection (MV DC) and control (AC to DCS/instruments) remain available for the full battery autonomy period, giving the operator visibility and the ability to operate the MV switchgear during a blackout.
Tip — SPOF on the DC path: A common finding is that both the UPS and the DCDB charger are supplied from the same LV bus section or even the same feeder, removing their independence. On site, verify that the UPS and DCDB have segregated sources. Also check the battery condition and last test date — an aged or untested battery is a hidden SPOF that does not show up on the SLD.
Key takeaway: Three paths — normal (utility to motors), standby (DG via ATS to essential loads), and DC/control (DCDB to MV switchgear; UPS to DCS). Trace all three on every SLD you review; the points where paths converge or where independence is lost are your SPOF candidates.

Equipment Schedule and Standards

Where you are in the workflow: the equipment schedule is the quantitative companion to the function table — use it during the desk study to frame your calculations and check nameplate data on site.

Indicative equipment schedule

The schedule below summarises the principal electrical equipment for the reference ~2 MW plant. All ratings are indicative and tentative; confirm against the actual plant documents and nameplate data.

Tag Equipment Indicative rating / specification IEC standard
MV-MET Utility metering cubicle 11 kV; tariff-grade CTs/VTs (class 0.2S); kWh + kVAh meter; per utility specification. IEC 62053; utility specification.
MV-SWGR MV metal-clad switchgear 11 kV; 630–1250 A bus (indicative); 25 kA / 1 s fault withstand; vacuum CBs; 4 panels (incomer, bus-section, 2 × transformer feeder). IEC 62271-200; IEC 62271-100.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor Resistance to limit earth-fault current to ~300–400 A (indicative); with neutral CT; outdoor or indoor housing. IEC 60099-6 (guidance); manufacturer's specification.
TX-1 / TX-2 MV/LV power transformers 2 × 1600 kVA (indicative); 11 kV / 0.4 kV; Dyn11; Z ≈ 6 %; cast-resin (dry-type) or oil-immersed (ONAN); off-load taps ±2 × 2.5 %. IEC 60076 series.
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC 400 V; split bus A/B; ~2500–3200 A main bus (indicative); 50–65 kA fault withstand; ACB incomers + motorised N.O. bus-tie ACB; MCCB outgoing feeders; Form 4b segregation. IEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2.
MCC-1 / MCC-2 Motor Control Centres 400 V; horizontal bus 1000–2000 A (indicative); 50 kA fault withstand; withdrawable or fixed starter buckets: DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD; MCCB or fuse-contactor, contactor, electronic overload/MPR; Form 3b/4b. IEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2.
VFD Variable Frequency Drives Rated per motor; 400 V input; input choke + EMC filter; integrated or remote keypad; RS485/PROFIBUS/PROFINET fieldbus interface. IEC 61800-3; IEC 61800-5.
PFC Power factor correction bank Automatic; detuned (7 % tuning reactor); sized to correct ~0.85 → 0.95+ (indicative); contactor-switched capacitor steps. IEC 60831; IEC 60831-1/-2.
DB Distribution boards 400/230 V; MCB and RCBO outgoing ways; surface or flush-mounted; IP rating per environment. IEC 61439-3.
EDB Essential distribution board 400/230 V; fed via ATS; rated for essential load; MCB/RCBO outgoing. IEC 61439-3.
DG Diesel generator set ~800–1250 kVA (indicative — sized by essential load list); 400 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz; AVR; fuel day-tank; acoustic canopy; auto-start panel. IEC 60034 (generator); ISO 8528 (genset performance).
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch Rated for essential bus full load; mechanically interlocked; open-transition or closed-transition (confirm per plant); changeover time per DG start time (indicative 10–30 s). IEC 60947-6-1.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply Online double-conversion; rated per DCS/instrument load; static bypass; battery autonomy 15–30 min (indicative); 400 V or 230 V single-phase output as required. IEC 62040-1; IEC 62040-3.
DCDB DC battery and charger system 110 V DC (typical for MV switchgear); float/boost charger; VRLA or vented lead-acid battery; battery sized for ~8 h autonomy (confirm per spec) plus interruption allowance. IEC 60896-11/-21; IEC 62485-2.

Applicable IEC standards summary

Equipment category Primary standard(s)
MV switchgear (metal-clad)IEC 62271-200; IEC 62271-100 (vacuum CBs)
Power transformersIEC 60076 series
LV switchgear and MCC assembliesIEC 61439-1; IEC 61439-2
LV circuit breakers (ACB, MCCB, MCB)IEC 60947-2; IEC 60947-3
MotorsIEC 60034 series
Variable speed drives / VFDsIEC 61800-3; IEC 61800-5
Power factor correction capacitorsIEC 60831
Uninterruptible power suppliesIEC 62040-1; IEC 62040-3
Automatic transfer switchesIEC 60947-6-1
DC battery systemsIEC 60896; IEC 62485
Earthing and bonding (general installation)IEC 60364 series
Lightning protection systemsIEC 62305 series
Protection relays / IEDsIEC 60255 series
FMECA methodologyIEC 60812
Reliability / availability (RAM)ISO 14224; ISO 20815
Arc-flash incident energyIEEE 1584 (US basis, widely adopted)
Note — standards confirmation: The standards listed are indicative. The governing standards for each plant are determined by the client specification and the applicable national regulations. Check the project specification and confirm with the client which revision of each standard applies.
Key takeaway: The equipment schedule gives you the quantitative framework for the desk study — use it to check whether the nameplate data you collect on site matches the design intent. Any significant discrepancy (wrong rating, different vector group, different impedance) is a calculation input that may change your load-flow or short-circuit result and should be noted in the Document Inventory and potentially the FMECA. Part 2 now shows you how to run the calculations.

Part 1 — Self-Check

Where you are in the workflow: test yourself before moving to the calculations in Part 2. If you cannot answer these confidently, re-read the relevant section of Part 1.

Attempt all six questions before revealing the answers. Use only the master SLD (Fig 1-1) and your memory — no notes.

Question 1

Which two tags mark the boundary between the 11 kV and the 400 V parts of the plant? State what each one does.

Show answer

TX-1 and TX-2 — the two MV/LV power transformers (1600 kVA each, indicative; 11 kV / 0.4 kV, Dyn11). They step the 11 kV utility supply down to 400 V for the LV distribution system. TX-1 feeds Bus A and TX-2 feeds Bus B of the LV-MSB. Everything above them is MV (11 kV); everything below them is LV (400 V).

Question 2

What is the difference between LV-MSB and MCC-1? Which one feeds which?

Show answer

LV-MSB is the PCC (Power Control Centre) / Main LV Switchboard — it receives the transformer outputs and distributes bulk 400 V power. Think "the LV power hub."

MCC-1 is a Motor Control Centre — an assembly of motor starters (DOL, soft-starter, VFD) that control and protect individual process motors. Think "where the motors are started."

The LV-MSB feeds MCC-1 (via an outgoing MCCB feeder from Bus A). MCC-1 feeds the individual motors.

Question 3

The bus-tie BT is normally open. What event causes it to close automatically, and why is that desirable?

Show answer

The bus-tie closes automatically on the trip or loss of either LV incomer ACB (i.e. loss of one transformer or its MV feeder). The auto-close logic detects the incomer trip and closes BT so the surviving transformer can carry both Bus A and Bus B loads.

This is desirable because it prevents loss of supply to half the plant whenever one transformer is lost — it is the primary N-1 resilience feature of the LV distribution. Without it, loss of TX-1 would immediately shed all Bus A loads (MCC-1, PFC, DB-A and any Bus A feeders).

Question 4

Name the three tags on the standby/essential power path and state the order in which energy flows through them on a utility outage.

Show answer

DG → ATS → EDB.

On a utility outage: the DG starts (auto-start signal from the ATS), ramps to rated voltage and frequency; the ATS transfers the essential bus from the utility source to the DG output; the EDB (essential distribution board) remains energised, supplying escape lighting, fire systems and critical process auxiliaries.

Question 5

Why does the plant need a separate DCDB when it already has a UPS? What does each supply?

Show answer

They serve two different and independently critical supply circuits:

  • UPS supplies AC power (400 V or 230 V) to the DCS, PLC, SCADA and instrumentation systems. These are AC-powered electronics that need a clean, regulated, uninterrupted supply.
  • DCDB supplies 110 V DC to the trip and close coils of the MV switchgear breakers and to the MV protection relay IEDs. These must operate even when the AC system is completely dead — a DC battery is the only supply that can guarantee this.

If you replaced the DCDB with a UPS, the MV switchgear would lose its ability to trip or close once the UPS itself lost its input feed. The DCDB's battery is the last line of protection operability during a full AC blackout.

Question 6

What is the purpose of MV-NER, and what would happen if it were removed (neutral left floating)?

Show answer

MV-NER (the Neutral Earthing Resistor) earths the 11 kV system neutral through a resistor to limit earth-fault current to approximately 300–400 A (indicative). This value is high enough for earth-fault protection relays to detect the fault reliably, but low enough to limit damage to equipment and reduce the step/touch voltage hazard at the fault location.

If the NER were removed and the neutral left floating (isolated neutral system):

  • An earth fault would not produce a reliable fault current — the protection relay could not detect it dependably, meaning the fault might persist and worsen.
  • The healthy phases would rise to line-to-line voltage above earth, stressing cable insulation and equipment rated for line-to-earth voltage.
  • Transient overvoltages (ferro-resonance) could develop on the unearthed network, potentially damaging voltage transformers and other equipment.
Key takeaway: If you answered all six correctly without hesitation, you are ready for Part 2 — Calculations. If any question exposed a gap, go back to the relevant section of Part 1 now. The calculations in Part 2 assume you know what every tag does and how the three power paths connect.

Part 2 — Calculations

Where you are in the workflow: you have identified the equipment and read the SLD (Part 1); now you run the numbers that tell you whether the existing equipment is correctly rated before you write a single recommendation.

Assessment calculations are verification, not green-field design. Your job in the field is to ask: "Is this transformer/breaker/cable/genset still adequate for what the plant is actually doing today?" — and to back the answer with arithmetic, not opinion.

Why Calculations Matter in an Assessment

Every rating you see on the SLD — a transformer kVA, a breaker breaking capacity, a busbar bracing figure, a cable cross-section, a capacitor bank, a genset — is the output of a calculation. That calculation is the audit trail that justifies the rating. In an assessment you work in the opposite direction: you start from the nameplate, reverse-engineer the assumption that was made, and check whether reality still matches it.

The two failure modes you are looking for:

  • Under-rating — cables and busbars that overheat (fire risk), switchgear that cannot interrupt a fault (catastrophic failure), protection that mis-coordinates or nuisance-trips, motors that stall, process voltage too low to run.
  • Over-rating — wasted capital tied up in oversized transformers, switchgear frames, cable and gensets the plant will never use; this matters most when the client is evaluating whether to add load or replace equipment.

The indicative design basis used throughout Part 2:

ParameterIndicative valueSource
Plant maximum demand~2.0 MWBasis of design
Power factor (uncorrected)0.85Basis of design
Apparent power~2.35 MVA2.0 / 0.85
Transformer configuration2 × 1600 kVA, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %Basis of design
LV voltage400 V, 3-phase, 50 HzIEC
MV voltage11 kVUtility connection
LV FLC per transformer≈ 2 310 A§c-flc
LV prospective Isc (single tx)≈ 38.5 kA§c-shortcircuit

Embed the master SLD here so every sub-section below can refer back to the equipment being sized.

Constants used throughout Part 2: √3 = 1.732  |  LV line voltage V = 400 V  |  MV line voltage = 11 kV  |  50 Hz. Every numeric result is indicative and tentative — it illustrates method and typical practice, not a substitute for project-specific load lists, short-circuit/load-flow studies, and the governing standards.

1 — Load & Demand Estimate

Goal: confirm the ~2 MW plant demand and convert it to apparent power (MVA) — the anchor point for every sizing calculation that follows.

Method

Assumptions (indicative): sum of connected loads (nameplate) ≈ 2.7 MW; overall demand/diversity factor ≈ 0.75; overall power factor before correction = 0.85.

Worked example — Demand and apparent power

Step 1 — Active demand:

Pdemand = Σ Pconnected × demand factor

Pdemand = 2.7 MW × 0.75 = 2.025 MW ≈ 2.0 MW

Step 2 — Apparent power:

S = P / pf

S = 2.0 MW / 0.85 = 2.35 MVA

Step 3 — Installed transformer base (with diversity/spare margin):

Sinstalled = 2.35 × 1.20 to 1.25 ≈ 2.8–3.0 MVA

This drives the 2 × 1600 kVA selection (3.2 MVA installed).

Result: maximum demand ≈ 2.0 MW / 2.35 MVA at 0.85 pf. All values indicative.

Field tip — What to capture: obtain the latest load list or motor schedule from the client. If none exists, read each MCC ammeter at peak shift and multiply by √3 × V × pf to back-calculate kW per feeder. Sum the branch demands to build your own diversity estimate.

Assessment interpretation

If as-found…Likely meaningAction
Demand > transformer capacity Plant has grown since original design Flag overload risk; recommend load-flow study
Demand < 50 % capacity for years Oversized original design or load reduction Check transformer no-load losses; note in report
No load list available Documentation gap Raise as finding; capture ammeter readings
The demand estimate sets the size of the entire electrical plant. Every other calculation in Part 2 is downstream of this number. If the load list is wrong or absent, all downstream ratings are unverifiable.

2 — Transformer Sizing & N-1 Check

Goal: verify the 2 × 1600 kVA selection and confirm that one transformer (TX-1 or TX-2) can carry the essential load via the bus-tie (BT) when the other is lost.

Normal loading

Worked example — Normal per-unit loading

With 2.35 MVA shared equally across both transformers:

Sper tx = 2.35 MVA / 2 = 1.175 MVA

Loadingnormal = 1.175 / 1.6 = 0.73 pu (≈ 73 %)

Each transformer runs at roughly 73 % of nameplate — a comfortable margin.

N-1 check (one transformer lost)

Worked example — N-1 loading with essential-load shedding

Worst case (no shedding):

LoadingN-1, full = 2.35 / 1.6 = 1.47 pu (147 %) — not acceptable for continuous operation.

With non-essential load shed (essential fraction ≈ 55 %, ≈ 0.65 MW / 1.0–1.3 MVA):

Sessential = 0.55 × 2.35 = 1.29 MVA (≈ 0.65 MW active at pf 0.8 → 0.81 MVA apparent running; includes 1.25 × step-load factor → 1.01 MVA for DG sizing — see §c-genset)

LoadingN-1, essential = 1.29 / 1.6 = 0.81 pu (≈ 81 %) — within continuous rating. ✓

Result: the 2 × 1600 kVA + bus-tie design is N-1 secure for essential load (~55 % of total, ≈ 0.65 MW / 1.29 MVA), provided non-essential load is shed automatically. This is the load-shedding requirement on LV-MSB. The same 0.65 MW essential-load basis is used for DG sizing in §c-genset.

Assessment check: confirm the bus-tie (BT) auto-transfer scheme is functional and the load-shedding sequence is current. A bus-tie that cannot close automatically, or load shedding that has never been tested, makes the N-1 claim invalid.

What to check on site

  • Transformer nameplates: confirm kVA, voltage ratio, vector group, Z %, off-load tap positions and current tap setting.
  • Transformer loading (incomer ammeter or SCADA trend): compare against 73 % normal / 81 % N-1 limits.
  • Bus-tie (BT): confirm auto-transfer is enabled and last test date.
  • Load-shedding relay/PMS: confirm essential/non-essential split list is documented and relays are active.
  • Transformer cooling: ONAN or ONAF? Are fans/pumps serviceable?
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory: populate the Transformer record row — enter nameplate kVA, Z %, vector group, tap position, and calculate loading % from the incomer ammeter reading.
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: transformer nameplate (kVA, Z %, voltage ratio, vector group, tap range and current position); incomer ACB ammeter reading (A) or SCADA kVA trend at peak load; bus-tie status (normally-open confirmed Y/N); load-shedding relay type and last-test date; cooling type (ONAN/ONAF) and fan status.
Two transformers are only as good as their bus-tie and load-shedding scheme. Verify all three on site before claiming N-1 security.

3 — Full-Load Current (LV & MV)

Goal: calculate the 400 V full-load current (FLC) of a 1600 kVA transformer — the basis for the incomer ACB frame size and busbar rating — and the corresponding MV primary current.

Formula

Ifl = S / (√3 × V)

Worked example — LV FLC (1600 kVA at 400 V)

Ifl = 1 600 000 VA / (1.732 × 400 V)

Ifl = 1 600 000 / 692.8 = 2 310 A

This sets the LV-MSB incomer ACB frame (≥ 2 500 A) and the ≈ 2 500–3 200 A busbar rating.

Worked example — MV FLC (1600 kVA at 11 kV)

Ifl,MV = 1 600 000 VA / (1.732 × 11 000 V)

Ifl,MV = 1 600 000 / 19 052 = 84 A

This sizes the MV transformer-feeder breakers [52-T1] / [52-T2] in MV-SWGR and the CT primary ratio for the feeder protection relays.

Quick sanity check on site: read the incomer ammeter or clamp-meter on the LV bus incomer cable. Divide by 2 310 A to get per-unit loading. Values consistently above 0.85 pu should be investigated.

General 3-phase FLC formula

For any three-phase equipment rated S (kVA) at line voltage V (V):

I = S × 1000 / (√3 × V)   [A]

Rating (kVA)VoltageFLC (A) indicative
1 600400 V2 310
1 60011 kV84
1 250400 V1 804
1 000400 V1 443
630400 V909
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory: populate the LV-MSB Incomer row — record ACB frame rating (A), current trip-unit setting (A), and measured FLC from ammeter (A); compare with calculated FLC to flag under- or over-rating.
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: transformer nameplate kVA; LV line voltage (measure with meter, do not rely on drawings); incomer ACB frame size and trip-unit In setting; busbar rated current (from board label or drawings); MV CT primary ratio on transformer feeder.
LV FLC ≈ 2 310 A per 1 600 kVA transformer at 400 V. Every conductor, breaker, and busbar on the LV incomer must be rated above this figure, with appropriate derating for installation conditions.

4 — Short-Circuit Current & Switchgear Withstand

Goal: calculate the LV prospective short-circuit current from the transformer impedance, check the bus-tie parallel case, and verify that the switchgear breaking capacity and busbar bracing are adequate.

Formula

Isc = Ifl / Z(pu)

Assumption: Z = 6 %; infinite upstream MV source (conservative — ignores MV source impedance, so the real value is slightly lower).

Worked example — Single-transformer fault contribution at 400 V

Isc = 2 310 A / 0.06 = 38 500 A ≈ 38.5 kA

This is the prospective symmetrical short-circuit current at the LV-MSB busbars from one transformer alone.

Worked example — Both transformers in parallel (bus-tie closed)

Isc,parallel ≈ 2 × 38.5 kA = 77 kA (upper bound; ignores source and cable impedance)

This is the bounding case when the bus-tie is closed and both transformers feed the same bus simultaneously.

Why the bus-tie is normally open

Operating the bus-tie (BT) normally-open keeps each bus half fed from only one transformer, limiting the fault duty on each section to ≈ 38.5 kA. Selecting the LV-MSB and MCC bus at 50–65 kA covers this single-transformer fault with margin. If the bus-tie is closed continuously (or accidentally left closed), the fault duty doubles to ≈ 77 kA and may exceed the board rating — a safety-critical finding.

Critical assessment check: if you find the bus-tie (BT) closed under normal running conditions, verify the switchboard fault rating immediately. A 50 kA-rated board exposed to 77 kA cannot safely interrupt a fault — this is a Category A finding requiring immediate action.

Site verification checklist

  • Confirm BT is normally-open and electrically interlocked (both incomers cannot close simultaneously onto the same bus).
  • Read ACB and MCCB nameplates: confirm rated breaking capacity (Icu) ≥ 38.5 kA (single-tx scenario).
  • Check busbar bracing rating on LV-MSB label or type-test certificate: should be ≥ 50 kA / 1 s (or equivalent peak).
  • Confirm a short-circuit/fault-level study exists and is dated within ≤ 5 years (or after any network change).
  • Check that MCCs fed from LV-MSB are rated for the same fault level.
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory / recommendations register: populate the Short-circuit Assessment row — record calculated Isc (kA), rated Icu on each ACB/MCCB (kA from nameplate), busbar bracing (kA from label), and the result (adequate / marginal / inadequate).
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: transformer nameplate Z % (each unit); ACB and MCCB breaking capacity (Icu) from nameplate or door label (kA); LV-MSB busbar short-time withstand rating (kA / duration) from board label or type-test certificate; bus-tie status and interlock scheme description; date of last formal fault-level study.
38.5 kA from one transformer; up to 77 kA with both in parallel. The normally-open bus-tie is a protective operating rule, not just a design convention — verify the interlock is active and the board rating has not been quietly exceeded.

5 — Motor FLC, Starting Methods & LV/MV Feed Threshold

Goal: calculate the full-load current and starting current of a representative LV motor, explain why large motors use soft-start or VFD, and identify the LV-to-MV feed decision threshold.

Full-load current formula (motor)

Ifl = P / (√3 × V × pf × η)

Worked example — 110 kW LV motor FLC and DOL starting current

Given: P = 110 kW, V = 400 V, pf = 0.86, η = 0.95.

FLC:

Ifl = 110 000 / (1.732 × 400 × 0.86 × 0.95)

Ifl = 110 000 / 566 = 194 A

DOL starting current (≈ 6.5 × FLC):

Istart = 6.5 × 194 = 1 260 A

A 1 260 A inrush causes a voltage dip on the bus, mechanical/electrical stress on the motor windings, and possible nuisance trips on other feeders. A soft-starter limits this to ≈ 3 × FLC (≈ 580 A); a VFD limits it to near FLC and provides speed control.

Starting method selection guide

Motor size (LV) Typical starting method Approx. start current Notes
Up to ≈ 7.5 kW Direct-on-line (DOL) ≈ 6–7 × FLC Bus impact negligible at this size
≈ 7.5–55 kW DOL / star-delta / soft-starter Star-delta: ≈ 2 × FLC Star-delta suits constant-torque loads; soft-starter preferred for pumps
≈ 55–200 kW Soft-starter or VFD Soft-starter: ≈ 3 × FLC Soft-start/VFD reduces bus dip and extends motor life
Speed-controlled loads VFD (any size) ≈ 1 × FLC Energy saving on pumps/fans (affinity laws)

LV-to-MV feed threshold

Individual motors larger than ≈ 150–200 kW are often more economically fed at MV (3.3 / 6.6 kV) via a dedicated MV motor starter rather than the 400 V MCC. At 200 kW, the LV current reaches ≈ 350 A — cable and starter costs rise steeply. For a ≈ 2 MW plant the largest drives may sit near this boundary; check the motor schedule.

Assessment tip: if you find a large motor (> 150 kW) on the LV MCC with no soft-start or VFD, check the voltage-dip impact on adjacent motors and instruments. Also check whether the MCC starter contactor and cable are correctly rated — the starting current can be 6–7 times the FLC nameplate.
For a 110 kW motor at 400 V: FLC ≈ 194 A, DOL inrush ≈ 1 260 A. Motors above ≈ 55 kW should have soft-start or VFD; above ≈ 150–200 kW, consider MV feed.

6 — Cable Sizing & Voltage Drop

Goal: size a motor feeder cable and verify the voltage drop is within acceptable limits (typically ≤ 5 % for motor feeders).

Four-step method

Worked example — 110 kW motor feeder (from §c-motors)

Step 1 — Design (base) current:

IB = motor FLC = 194 A

Step 2 — Derating factors (IEC 60364):

kgrouping = 0.80 (cables grouped on tray)

ktemp = 0.94 (40 °C ambient)

ktotal = 0.80 × 0.94 = 0.752

Iz(req) = IB / ktotal = 194 / 0.752 = 258 A

Step 3 — Cable selection:

Select 95 mm² Cu XLPE with tabulated rating ≈ 264–300 A on tray (per manufacturer / IEC tables). Iz ≥ 258 A. ✓

Step 4 — Voltage-drop check:

Run length L = 120 m; for 95 mm² Cu: R ≈ 0.23 Ω/km, X ≈ 0.08 Ω/km; pf = 0.86 → cosφ = 0.86, sinφ = 0.51.

ΔV = √3 × I × (R·cosφ + X·sinφ) × L

ΔV = 1.732 × 194 × (0.23 × 0.86 + 0.08 × 0.51) × 0.120

ΔV = 1.732 × 194 × (0.1978 + 0.0408) × 0.120

ΔV = 1.732 × 194 × 0.2386 × 0.120 = 9.6 V

%ΔV = 9.6 / 400 × 100 = 2.4 %

Within the ≤ 5 % limit (and ≤ 3 % is comfortable). Selection accepted.

Assessment guidance

In the field you cannot always measure cable resistance directly. Use the cable size stamped on the gland tag or tray label, the feeder length from the cable schedule, and the above formula to estimate voltage drop. Then compare with the measured voltage at the motor terminal (meter at the motor DB) versus the LV-MSB bus voltage.

Undersized cable signs: discoloured insulation, hot gland or termination, cable tray showing signs of heat distortion, motor terminal voltage significantly below bus voltage. Any of these should be flagged immediately.

Common derating factors (indicative — confirm against project installation tables)

FactorConditionIndicative k value
Grouping (kg)3 cables on tray0.90
Grouping (kg)6 cables on tray0.80
Grouping (kg)9 cables on tray0.73
Ambient (kt)35 °C0.97
Ambient (kt)40 °C0.94
Ambient (kt)45 °C0.91
Burial depthStandard 0.7 m direct burial1.00
Soil thermal resistivityHigh (dry/sandy)0.80–0.85
Cable sizing = thermal capacity (derating) + voltage drop. Both checks are mandatory. A cable that is thermally adequate but drops too much voltage leaves motors unable to start or run at rated torque.

7 — Power-Factor Correction (PFC)

Goal: size the automatic capacitor bank (PFC) to raise the plant power factor from 0.85 to 0.95, and understand why detuning reactors are mandatory when VFDs are present.

Formula

Qc = P × (tanφ1 − tanφ2)

Worked example — PFC from 0.85 to 0.95 (P = 2.0 MW)

pf1 = 0.85 → φ1 = 31.8°, tanφ1 = 0.620

pf2 = 0.95 → φ2 = 18.2°, tanφ2 = 0.329

Qc = 2 000 kW × (0.620 − 0.329) = 2 000 × 0.291 = 582 kVAr

Select a standard ≈ 600 kVAr automatic bank in steps (e.g. 6 × 100 kVAr).

Correcting to 0.95 also reduces apparent power: S = 2.0 / 0.95 = 2.11 MVA (from 2.35 MVA), relieving transformer and cable loading by ≈ 10 %.

Detuning reactors

VFDs and non-linear loads inject harmonic currents (5th, 7th, 11th… harmonics). An un-detuned capacitor bank presents a very low impedance at harmonic frequencies and can resonate with the supply inductance, causing catastrophic capacitor failure and high harmonic distortion. For this plant, with multiple VFDs, the bank must be fitted with 7 % detuning reactors (series inductors that raise the bank's resonant frequency above the 5th harmonic).

Assessment check: if you find a capacitor bank without detuning reactors on a plant with VFDs, this is a reliability finding. Check for capacitor over-temperature, bulging, or blown fuses in the bank — signs that resonance has already caused damage.

What to record on site

  • Confirm PFC bank kVAr rating matches ≈ 600 kVAr (± 20 % acceptable).
  • Confirm detuning reactors (7 % or 14 %) are present and intact.
  • Read power-factor meter at LV-MSB (or from the SCADA historian trend): should be ≥ 0.93 with bank in service.
  • Check power-factor penalty charges on the utility invoice — evidence of consistently low pf even with the bank in service suggests under-sizing or damaged capacitors.
  • Note the automatic controller step count and switching threshold settings.
~580 kVAr of correction raises pf from 0.85 to 0.95, reducing apparent power by ≈ 10 %. On a VFD-heavy plant, detuning reactors are not optional — they are the difference between a working bank and an exploding one.

8 — Standby Generator Sizing Rationale

Goal: justify the indicative DG rating (≈ 1 000 kVA) on an essential-load basis and explain the constraints that really drive the size.

Sizing calculation

Worked example — Essential-load generator sizing

Assumptions (indicative): the DG covers essential loads only (not full 2 MW plant); essential active load ≈ 0.65 MW at pf 0.8.

Running (steady) apparent demand:

Sessential = P / pf = 0.65 MW / 0.8 = 0.81 MVA

Step-load / motor-start allowance (the largest essential motor starting against the generator's limited transient capacity):

SDG ≥ Sessential × 1.25 = 0.81 × 1.25 = 1.01 MVA

Result: select a ≈ 1 000 kVA standby DG (indicative band: 800–1250 kVA per equipment schedule; 1000 kVA is the worked point value) — consistent with the N-1 essential-load basis of 0.65 MW / ~1.29 MVA in §c-transformer. All values indicative.

The governing constraint: voltage dip on motor start

For a generator, the largest single motor start is often the governing constraint, not the steady running load. A generator has a much higher source impedance than the utility, so a large DOL motor start causes a severe transient voltage dip that can stall other motors already running. The remedy is:

  • Soft-start or VFD all large essential motors (reduces starting kVA to ≈ 1.0–1.5 × FLC).
  • Sequence essential motor starts after DG reaches rated voltage/frequency (ATS timer).
  • Size the DG AVR for fast voltage recovery (< 2 s to within ± 3 %).
De-rating reminders: genset ratings are typically at ISO conditions (25 °C, sea level). For hot climates or elevated sites, apply the manufacturer's de-rating factor (commonly −1 % per 5 °C above 25 °C, −1 % per 100 m above sea level). A 1 000 kVA unit at 45 °C and 300 m may deliver only ≈ 940 kVA — check the site data sheet.

Site assessment checklist

  • Confirm DG nameplate kVA rating and compare with essential-load estimate.
  • Check de-rating data sheet for site altitude and ambient temperature.
  • Confirm ATS (ATS) transfer time setting and load-shed sequence at DG start.
  • Check fuel day-tank level and fuel consumption log (evidence of recent tests).
  • Review last monthly/annual test run report: confirm the DG accepted the full essential load at rated voltage and frequency.
  • Confirm AVR and governor are within calibration.
  • Check battery/charger for engine starting: voltage, last replacement date.
The genset is not sized for full plant load — only the essential fraction (≈ 55 %, ≈ 1 000 kVA). The motor-start transient is usually the binding constraint, not the steady-state load. A genset that has never been tested under full essential load cannot be claimed as reliable backup.

9 — Reading & Validating a Load-Flow Study

Goal: confirm the voltage profile, power flows and losses at peak load — that every bus stays within ± 5 % of nominal and no branch is thermally overloaded.

Transformer voltage regulation (hand check)

Worked example — Voltage regulation at 73 % loading

Split Z = 6 % into R ≈ 1.2 %, X ≈ 5.9 %. At normal ≈ 73 % loading (§c-transformer), pf ≈ 0.90 (cosφ = 0.90, sinφ = 0.436):

ΔV% ≈ loadpu × (R%·cosφ + X%·sinφ)

ΔV% ≈ 0.73 × (1.2 × 0.90 + 5.9 × 0.436)

ΔV% ≈ 0.73 × (1.08 + 2.57) = 0.73 × 3.65 = 2.7 %

With a +2.5 % off-load tap, the LV-MSB bus sits at ≈ 400 V at full load. Adding the worst feeder drop of 2.4 % (§c-cables) puts the motor terminal at ≈ 97–98 % of nominal — inside the ± 5 % limit. ✓

Indicative transformer losses

Transformer copper (load) loss scales with loading2. For a unit with full-load copper loss ≈ 17 kW at 73 % loading:

PCu = 0.732 × 17 kW = 0.533 × 17 ≈ 9 kW per transformer at normal load.

Iron (no-load) loss is constant; for a 1 600 kVA unit, typically ≈ 2–3 kW.

How to read a load-flow report

Report output What to check Acceptable limit
Bus voltage (pu or V) All buses at peak load ± 5 % of nominal (IEC 60364 / utility spec)
Transformer loading (%) Each unit in normal / N-1 ≤ 100 % continuous; ≤ 120 % emergency (IEC 60076 short-time)
Feeder branch loading (%) All cables and busbars ≤ 100 % of rated ampacity
Reactive flow (MVAr) Power factor at PCC Target ≥ 0.95 with PFC in service
Losses (kW) Transformer Cu + Fe losses Flag if > 2 % of transformer rating
If no load-flow study exists: this is itself a finding. A plant with no documented power-system study has no formal basis for its tap settings, cable ratings, or transformer loading limits. Recommend a load-flow study as part of your remediation programme.
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory: record date and revision of the load-flow study; confirm worst-case bus voltage (pu), peak transformer loading (%), and whether any branch exceeded rated ampacity. If no study exists, raise as a documentation gap.
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: latest load-flow study report (or SCADA trend data for bus voltages and feeder amps at peak); transformer tap position (from nameplate tap switch / HV tap changer setting); measured LV bus voltage (V) at peak shift; power-factor reading at main incomer; any voltage complaints from process.
A load-flow study validates the sizing from §1–§8 under realistic load conditions. Without it you cannot formally claim that voltages, flows, and taps are correct. Its absence is a finding, not just a gap.

10 — Protection Coordination & Selectivity

Goal: set protective devices so the device closest to the fault trips first, isolating the smallest possible zone (selectivity/discrimination), while still clearing every fault within the required time.

The protection chain

Tracing upstream from a fault on a motor feeder:

Motor MCCB → MCC busbar → LV outgoing MCCB → LV incomer ACB (LV-MSB) → MV transformer-feeder relay [52-T] (MV-SWGR) → MV incomer relay [52-I]

Time grading

Worked example — Time-graded relay settings (indicative)

Between two graded relays, leave a margin Δt for breaker opening time + relay overshoot + CT error + safety ≈ 0.3 s:

tupstream = tdownstream + Δt

Indicative ladder (from feeder to incomer):

Device / relaySetting (indicative)Notes
Motor feeder MCCB ≈ 0.05 s (instantaneous/short-time) Closest to fault; fastest clear
LV incomer ACB short-time delay t = 0.20 s 0.05 + Δt ≈ 0.20 s
MV feeder relay [52-T] (IDMT 51) t = 0.50 s 0.20 + 0.30 = 0.50 s
MV incomer relay [52-I] t = 0.80 s 0.50 + 0.30 = 0.80 s

Worked example — MV relay pickup setting (CT 100/1 on 84 A primary FLC)

Ipickup = 1.25 × 84 A = 105 A → 1.05 × CT (set ≈ 1.05 on a 100/1 CT)

Transformer internal faults are cleared instantaneously by the 87T differential relay, independent of the time grading above.

Assessment: what to verify

  • Request the coordination study / time-current coordination curves (TCC). If none exists, raise as a finding.
  • Confirm relay settings in the IED configuration files match the approved coordination study values.
  • Confirm each protective device (ACB, MCCB, relay) has been tested within the required period (typically every 1–3 years for MV relays; 5 years for LV trip units).
  • Check that the 87T differential relay (if fitted) is in service and its CTs are correctly wired and matched.
  • Confirm IDMT curve type and time-multiplier setting are consistent across all upstream relays.
  • Verify zone-selective interlocking (ZSI) or bus-differential is active (if the design relies on it for arc-flash mitigation).
Loss of selectivity means an upstream breaker trips for a downstream fault, blacking out a much larger zone than the faulty feeder. This is a high-impact finding — it multiplies the production loss from any fault. It is also invisible until a fault occurs unless the coordination curves are checked.
On the forms →
(a) Document inventory / recommendations register: record whether a coordination study (TCC) exists and its date; note the LV incomer ACB short-time setting (s) and MV feeder relay time-dial / time-multiplier setting; flag any device whose as-found setting does not match the study.
(b) AS-FOUND site data required: relay settings reports or IED configuration printouts for each MV feeder relay, MV incomer relay, and transformer differential relay; ACB trip-unit settings (Long-time, Short-time, Instantaneous bands and delays in amps and seconds); CT ratios on each MV feeder; last relay/trip-unit test date and result.
A coordination study tells you that relay A trips before relay B for a fault between them. Without documented, verified settings, you cannot make that claim — and the next fault will reveal the gap.

11 — Arc-Flash / Incident Energy (IEEE 1584) & the Clearing-Time Lever

Goal: estimate the incident energy and arc-flash boundary at the LV-MSB, label the gear and select PPE — and show why clearing time is the dominant lever for reducing arc-flash hazard.

Arc-flash is a life-safety calculation. The indicative values below illustrate method only. Actual PPE selection, labelling, and arc mitigation must be based on a full IEEE 1584-2018 study with site-specific data. Never rely on these figures for live-work decisions.

Inputs for the LV-MSB

Input parameterIndicative valueSource
Bolted fault current Ibf38.5 kA §c-shortcircuit
System voltage400 VBasis of design
Enclosure typeSwitchboard (MCC-type)IEEE 1584-2018
Working distance D600 mmIEEE 1584 default LV switchboard
Upstream device clearing time t0.20 s (LV incomer short-time delay) §c-protection

Worked example — Indicative arc-flash calculation at LV-MSB (400 V)

Step 1 — Arcing current (LV arcing/bolted ratio ≈ 0.58 per IEEE 1584):

Ia ≈ 0.58 × 38.5 kA ≈ 22 kA

Step 2 — Incident energy at D = 600 mm, t = 0.20 s (indicative from IEEE 1584-2018 empirical model):

E ≈ 10 cal/cm² (assumed illustrative value — not computed from the full six-parameter IEEE 1584-2018 model; use recognised power-system software for labelling studies)

(This is an illustration only — the IEEE 1584-2018 model uses six parameters and electrode configuration corrections that cannot be reduced to a simple formula here.)

Step 3 — Arc-flash boundary (distance where E falls to EB = 1.2 cal/cm², inverse-square scaling):

AFB ≈ D × √(E / EB)

AFB ≈ 0.6 m × √(10 / 1.2) = 0.6 × 2.89 ≈ 1.7 m
