7 · Sign-off Prepared-by (engineer name, organisation, date); acknowledgement by the host escort (name, signature or email confirmation); PM review date (leave blank until PM signs). Obtain escort acknowledgement on the day, even if only by email confirmation.

Photo selection for the report

Include in SF-04 all 12 mandatory photos plus any additional photos that directly support an observation in the findings table. The following photos carry the most evidential weight and should always be included if captured:

  • Bus-tie breaker position indicator — confirms normal operating mode (open or closed).
  • Arc-flash label (or its absence) — supports the PPE / incident-energy FMECA finding.
  • DC / battery charger condition — supports the latent DC-supply SPOF finding.
  • Earth test point with date tag — confirms when the earthing system was last tested.
  • Any visible defect: tracking on cable terminations, corrosion on connections, dust-clogged VFD filters, missing fire-protection equipment — each defect photo must link to an observation row.

Tone and accuracy

  • Factual, not inferential. SF-04 records what you observed; the inference ("this is a SPOF because…") belongs in SF-02 FMECA. Example: Good SF-04 text: "Bus-tie CB confirmed by escort as manually operated only; no automatic transfer relay fitted." Not for SF-04: "The manual bus-tie is a critical single point of failure."
  • Attribute verbal information to the escort. If the plant supervisor tells you something that is not visible on a nameplate or SLD (e.g. "we last tested the battery three years ago"), attribute it: "Confirmed by [Escort Name] on [date]".
  • Report absences explicitly. "No arc-flash label observed on MV-SWGR incomer cubicle" is a valid finding. Do not omit negative observations.
Key takeaway: SF-04 is a factual record, not a risk assessment. Write it within 48 hours of the visit. Every FMECA row must trace to a SF-04 observation or a SF-01 gap. The photo log is mandatory — index all photos the same day they are taken.

Case Study — Jubail SWRO Desalination Plant

Where you are in the workflow: the methods are taught; now see all four forms filled in for one complete plant.

Introduction

The embedded document below is a simulated, end-to-end example showing all four assessment forms completed for a single imagined plant: the Jubail Seawater Reverse Osmosis (SWRO) desalination plant (reference P01). It has been deliberately constructed to exercise every form section and to illustrate the full range of SPOF types, priorities, and recommendation categories that an engineer is likely to encounter across the 21-plant campaign.

Scope disclosure — why the Jubail plant is larger than the manual's reference basis. The Jubail SWRO study is a deliberately larger, simulated plant designed to show that the assessment method scales beyond the manual's indicative ~2 MW / 11 kV / 400 V reference. The Jubail plant uses:
  • 132 kV grid incomers (not 11 kV)
  • 2 × 40 MVA 132/13.8 kV transformers (not 2 × 1600 kVA 11/0.4 kV)
  • 13.8 kV main switchboard (not 400 V LV-MSB)
The method — SPOF tracing, FMECA scoring on a 1–5 scale, priority banding — is identical. The scale difference simply exercises the forms on a larger system.

Because the Jubail plant is a distinct, larger facility, the document uses a site-specific extended asset-tag taxonomy (P01- prefix) rather than the manual's approved core tag scheme. The table below maps the principal Jubail site tags to their approved-scheme equivalents for cross-reference:
Jubail site tag Approved core tag equivalent Equipment
P01-SWGR-01MV-SWGRMain HV/MV switchgear
P01-BUSTIE-01BTBus-tie circuit breaker
P01-GEN-01DGStandby / emergency generator
P01-DC-01DCDBDC distribution board / battery
P01-TX-01 / P01-TX-02TX-1 / TX-2Main power transformers
P01-UPS-01UPSUninterruptible power supply
P01-ATS-01ATSAutomatic transfer switch
P01-MCC-01 / P01-MCC-02MCC-1 / MCC-2Motor control centres
The manual's approved core tag scheme (MV-MET, MV-SWGR, MV-PROT, MV-NER, TX-1/2, LV-MSB, BT, MCC-1…n, VFD, PFC, DG, ATS, UPS, DCDB, DB, EDB) continues to govern all standard deliverables produced by this campaign.
Note — simulated data. Every value, observation, equipment tag, document reference, personnel name, and cost band in this study is fictitious. Data items are tagged SIM-… throughout to confirm their illustrative-only status. Nothing in this document represents real Saudi Water Authority asset data, real equipment condition, or a real cost estimate. The study has been built to demonstrate the method — not to describe a real plant. All costs shown are indicative bands only (L / M / H) and must never be cited as a quote or an estimate.

Reading guide

Use the table below to navigate between the four embedded forms and the manual sections that taught the underlying method. Read each form, then return to the referenced section to confirm you understand why the form looks the way it does.

Form What it demonstrates Taught in
SF-01 — Document Inventory 15 documents logged; 7 Received, 6 Partial, 2 Missing, 1 Obsolete. Shows how document gaps (Missing DGA, Missing battery test, Obsolete arc-flash study) directly feed FMECA Detection ratings. The gap summary is the data-call agenda. Desk Study Procedure (this Part)
SF-02 — FMECA 16 failure-mode rows covering the full spectrum: SPOF bus-tie, protection selectivity gap, DC supply latent failure (top RPN = 60), arc-flash PPE boundary, single service transformer SPOF, earthing untested, genset hidden failure. Three SPOF=Y rows; priority derivation from RPN and SPOF flags demonstrated. Part 3 — FMECA Method; Part 3 — SPOF Checklist
SF-03 — Recommendations 16 recommendations closing the loop from every FMECA row. Shows the mechanical priority rule, the cost-band method, and the distinction between modification recommendations and monitor-only findings. Dependency / sequencing column demonstrates how recommendations can create critical-path constraints. Part 4 — Modifications Catalogue
SF-04 — Site Visit Report Full MOM, 12 observation rows each linked to an FM-ID, 10 photo-log entries, QHSE notes. Demonstrates the factual (non-inferential) tone and the traceability chain: observation → FM-ID → REC-ID. Writing the Site Visit Report (this Part)

Jubail SWRO study — all four forms

Peer-review commentary — what an experienced engineer notices

What an experienced engineer notices when reviewing this study

The following observations are the kind of quality-check commentary that a senior reviewer or the PM would raise when reviewing the Jubail study. Use them as a self-check when you produce your own forms.

  • The highest RPN row (FM-006, DC charger, RPN = 60) is rated P1 immediately — not because the occurrence is high, but because the Detection rating is 5 (hidden). The DC supply feeds the protection and trip circuits; a failure that is undetected until a fault occurs is the most dangerous class of latent SPOF. The study correctly assigns "Immediate" urgency and cross-links to the missing battery capacity-discharge test in SF-01. On your own plants, always check whether the DC charger / battery-test record is in SF-01 before assigning a D rating — if it is missing, D = 5, and the row will likely be P1.
  • The protection-selectivity finding (FM-002, RPN = 36) exploits the document gap in SF-01 (P01-DOC-03 Partial, pre-dates expansion) to justify a high Detection = 4. This is the correct method: a protection study that predates a plant modification is not evidence that grading is correct — it is evidence that grading is unknown. The study appropriately flags this as Partial in SF-01 and elevates D accordingly. When you see a protection study dated before a major expansion on any of your plants, treat grading as unverified until proved otherwise.
  • Three SPOF=Y rows are identified: FM-001 (manual bus-tie), FM-005 (single service transformer), and FM-012 (common MV bus). Notice the difference in severity: FM-001 and FM-012 carry S = 4 (significant production impact), while FM-005 carries S = 3 (sub-area only, not on RO critical path). Priority is assigned accordingly (FM-001 and FM-012 → P1; FM-005 → P2). When you identify a SPOF on your plants, always ask: does loss of this single component cause a plant-wide outage (S = 4 or 5) or only a partial / sub-system outage (S = 2 or 3)? The answer sets the priority class.
  • The recommendation for the manual bus-tie (REC-01) specifies "add automatic bus-tie transfer scheme with interlocks" — not just "close the bus-tie". Simply closing the bus-tie permanently would eliminate the N+1 architectural benefit. The correct fix is an automatic transfer scheme that closes the tie on a monitored event (transformer loss or incomer undervoltage) and re-opens it once the fault is cleared. The study correctly notes that grading must be revalidated in parallel, because closing the bus-tie changes the fault-current contribution seen by each relay.
  • The earthing finding (FM-014) carries a moderate RPN (24) but a note that the last test was in 2013 — 13 years prior to the study date. The study correctly escalates the Detection rating on the basis that no re-test has been performed since the original commissioning. Corrosion in a coastal (saline) environment like Jubail is significantly faster than in an inland site, making the time-since-test argument even stronger. On coastal plants, any earthing test older than five years should be treated as Partial or Obsolete in SF-01.
  • Seven of the 16 recommendation rows are "monitor — no modification". This is disciplined use of the recommendations register: not every FMECA finding requires a capital modification. Items with low RPN and adequate existing safeguards are closed with a monitoring action and a cost band of L. A recommendations register that lists modifications for every single failure mode is a sign of under-calibrated FMECA scoring, not thoroughness.
  • The VFD cooling-filter dust loading (O-08 in SF-04) seeds FM-008, but the recommendation is to clean and replace filters rather than a capital modification. The study correctly distinguishes between an O&M action (P3, cost band L) and a capital modification. On your own plants, always separate the "do now — O&M" actions from the "invest in — capital" recommendations, and be explicit about which is which in SF-03.
  • SF-04 Observation O-04 (arc-flash study predates switchgear upgrade) appears in Section 6 (Safety notes) as well as in the findings table. This is correct practice: an unverified arc-flash boundary is both a FMECA finding (FM-004) and a QHSE concern that must be documented separately in the safety section of SF-04. On your own site visit reports, always copy any arc-flash label absence or outdated study finding into the QHSE section as well as the findings table.
  • The study's cost-band summary (L/M/H = 9/6/1) is stated explicitly in the SF-03 footer. This is useful for the client's budget planning. On your own plants, include this summary line in the SF-03 footer — it helps the PM aggregate across all 21 plants for the campaign summary report.
Key takeaway: Read the Jubail study as a worked example of the complete method — not as a template to copy blindly. The peer-review commentary above identifies the reasoning behind the scoring decisions. Apply that reasoning, not just the scores, on your own plants.

First-Assignment Walk-Through

Where you are in the workflow: you have received your first plant assignment. Follow this day-by-day sequence from first notification to submission.

This walk-through narrates a typical plant cycle from Day −5 (desk study begins) to Day 4 (quality assurance and submission). The timeline is indicative — a simple plant may compress to five days total; a complex plant with many document gaps may expand to eight or ten. Adjust as needed, but keep the sequence.

Day −5 — Desk study begins

Morning — receive the plant package and issue the document request

  1. Open the four blank assessment forms from Appendix — Blank Forms. Fill in the header of each form: plant name, plant reference number, engineer name, date, and status ("In preparation"). Save as working copies with the plant reference in the filename.
  2. Issue the document request to the plant O&M contact using the standard request template. Request all ten document categories listed in the Desk Study section. Log each requested item in SF-01 with status "Requested".
  3. Obtain the logistics information: site address, visitor access procedure, site safety induction requirements, escort contact details, and whether a PTW must be pre-arranged. Block the site visit date in your calendar.

Afternoon — first document review

  1. As documents arrive, update SF-01 status (Received / Partial). If documents are already available in the campaign document store, retrieve them immediately.
  2. Study the master SLD first. Trace the power path: utility → MV-MET → MV-SWGR → TX-1/TX-2 → LV-MSB (Bus A/B with bus-tie BT) → MCC-1 … MCC-n, VFD, DG/ATS, UPS/DCDB. Annotate: how many transformers? Is the bus-tie N/O? Is there an auto-transfer scheme? Any single-feed sub-areas?
  3. On a printed copy of the SLD, mark with a red pencil every component whose failure you believe might cause a plant-wide or significant partial outage. These are your preliminary SPOF candidates — you will verify them on site and refine them in the FMECA.

Day −2 — Desk calculations and pre-visit preparation

Morning — desk calculations

With the equipment schedule and load list in hand, build the calculation model shell and run the following desk-based checks (see Part 2 for all methods):

  • Transformer sizing check (Part 2): total active load (kW) ÷ assumed power factor → apparent power (kVA); divide by number of transformers; compare to installed transformer kVA. If >80 % loaded in normal operation, flag as a SPOF candidate.
  • FLC check (Part 2): for the largest motor on each MCC, calculate rated FLC; compare to MCC overload relay setting from the schedule. Note any that appear set ≥ 115 % FLC.
  • Indicative short-circuit level (Part 2): using transformer impedance and utility fault level (if known), estimate the maximum fault current at the LV main busbar. Compare to switchgear rated fault-withstand current. If utility fault level is not yet available, note this as a data-call.
  • Genset sizing check (Part 2): sum the essential-load list; compare to DG rated kVA at the assumed power factor. Confirm whether the DG covers full production or essential loads only. If the load list has gaps, note as a site question.

Afternoon — pre-visit preparation

  1. Print two A3 copies of the master SLD. Label one "RED-LINE COPY" and one "REFERENCE COPY". On the red-line copy, mark all preliminary SPOF candidates with a pencilled circle.
  2. Print the Visual Inspection Checklist and the Drawing Availability and SLD Verification Checklist from this manual. These are your primary field tools.
  3. Print a blank SF-04 Site Visit Report header and MOM template. Fill in the plant name, date, and visit agenda.
  4. Confirm with the escort: PTW for switchroom entry (confirm time), induction requirements, photography restrictions, and any areas that will not be accessible.
  5. Check PPE: arc-rated coveralls, hard hat, safety glasses, safety shoes, and gloves (confirm PPE category required by the PTW if known in advance).

Day 0 — Arrival and QHSE induction

On arrival

  1. Report to the plant control room or administration office. Sign in. Confirm the escort is present and the site induction is ready.
  2. Complete the site QHSE induction: emergency muster points, emergency contact numbers, permit-to-work procedure, specific hazards (process plant hazards are in addition to electrical hazards — confirm chemical, pressure, and process risks with the escort).
  3. Collect the PTW for switchroom entry. Read it in full. Confirm the scope, validity period, and conditions. Sign it.
  4. Attend the kick-off meeting with the escort and any other O&M staff available. Confirm the visit agenda, areas to be walked, and documents not yet received. Record attendees in SF-04 Section 2 (MOM). Confirm in writing (meeting minutes or email) the key verbal facts: bus-tie operating philosophy, genset scope, last arc-flash study date.

Day 1 — Site visit

Morning — MV and transformer walk-down

  1. Enter the MV switchroom under the PTW with the escort. Work through Zones 1–3 of the Visual Inspection Checklist: switchroom general, MV-SWGR / MV-MET / MV-NER, then TX-1 and TX-2.
  2. Photograph PH-01 (switchroom general), PH-02 (MV switchgear line-up and arc-flash labels), PH-03 (bus-tie position indicator), PH-04 (TX-1 nameplate), PH-05 (TX-2 nameplate) from the mandatory photo list.
  3. On the red-line SLD, confirm every item visible in the switchroom. Mark confirmed items with a tick; mark discrepancies in red. Note the as-found breaker positions.
  4. Ask the escort specifically: When was the protection coordination study last updated? Has the plant been expanded since that date? When was the arc-flash study last performed? Note answers in SF-04 Section 2.

Afternoon — LV, standby, DC, and earthing walk-down

  1. Work through Zones 4–9 of the Visual Inspection Checklist: LV-MSB and BT; MCCs and VFDs; PFC and DBs; DG and ATS; UPS and DCDB; visible earthing.
  2. Capture remaining mandatory photographs: PH-06 (LV-MSB), PH-07 (DG), PH-08 (ATS), PH-09 (DCDB), PH-10 (UPS), PH-11 (earth test point), PH-12 (any defect or discrepancy).
  3. Collect any documents not previously received: relay setting records, genset test log, battery test record, asset register pages. Log each new document in SF-01.
  4. Before leaving site: review your checklist — have all mandatory photographs been taken? Have all zones been walked? Are there open questions for the escort that can still be answered today? Ask them now.
  5. Agree the data-call action list with the escort (documents to be sent within agreed timescales). Record in SF-04 Section 2, decisions and actions table.

Days 2–3 — Analysis and form completion

Day 2 — SF-04, SF-01, FMECA build

  1. Finalise SF-04 first — while the visit is fresh. Complete all sections: metadata, MOM, survey scope, observations (one row per finding with tag and FM-ID), photo log (index and caption all photos), safety notes, and sign-off. Target: completed within 24 hours of the site visit.
  2. Update SF-01: change the status of any documents collected on site; add any new documents to the register; update the gap summary. Mark any remaining Missing or Partial items as data-call actions.
  3. Build the FMECA (SF-02): open a row for each SF-04 observation and for each SF-01 gap that represents a failure mode. Assign S, O, D; compute RPN and Crit; flag SPOFs. Cross-check: every SF-04 observation with an FM-ID should have a corresponding FMECA row. See Part 3 — FMECA Method for the S/O/D scale anchors.
  4. Update D ratings for all FMECA rows affected by SF-01 gaps (as described in the Post-Site Analysis section).

Day 3 — Calculations update and SF-03 recommendations

  1. Update the calculation model with the as-found nameplate data from the site visit. Re-run the transformer sizing, FLC, short-circuit, genset, and load-flow checks. Note any calculations that cannot be completed without outstanding data-call responses — flag these clearly in the calculation package.
  2. Build SF-03 Recommendations: one row per FM-ID from the FMECA. Assign priority mechanically from the RPN / SPOF rule. Write the recommended modification or action. Assign cost band (L/M/H). Note monitor-only rows explicitly. See Part 4 — Modifications Catalogue for standard modification types and their descriptions.
  3. Cross-check the traceability chain: every SF-03 row should carry an FM-ID from SF-02; every SF-02 row should carry a reference to an SF-04 observation or SF-01 gap. If any row is untraceable, either remove it or add the missing link.

Day 4 — Quality assurance and submission

  1. Self-check all four forms using this list:
    • SF-01: every row has a Status; gap summary count is correct; all data-call actions have owners and due dates.
    • SF-02: every row has S, O, D, RPN, Crit, and SPOF flag; every SPOF=Y row has a P1 or P2 recommendation in SF-03; no RPN cell is blank.
    • SF-03: every FM-ID from SF-02 has exactly one recommendation row; priority is consistent with the RPN/SPOF rule; cost band is L/M/H (no SAR figures).
    • SF-04: all seven sections are complete; MOM action list has owners and due dates; all 12 mandatory photos are indexed; escort acknowledgement received.
    • Calculation package: all required studies completed or flagged as pending data; indicative label on all values.
    • Traceability check: any FMECA row that cannot be traced to either an SF-04 observation or an SF-01 gap has been reviewed and either removed or sourced.
  2. Send the completed forms and calculation package to the PM for review. Attach the indexed photo library. Note any items that are still outstanding (pending data-call responses) and their expected resolution dates.
  3. File the red-lined SLD with the plant document package. Note any SLD revision that the plant owner needs to carry out, and reference this in SF-03 as an action (if applicable).
You are now ready. You have completed one full plant cycle: desk study → site visit → analysis → recommendations → submission. Repeat this sequence for each of the 21 plants in the campaign, adjusting the timeline as plant complexity and document availability dictate. Blank forms for every new plant are in Appendix — Blank Forms.
Note: These appendices are quick-reference and printable tools — concise distillations of the manual's technical content intended for use in the field and at the desk. All figures are indicative; confirm against plant-specific data and the governing standards before acting on them.

A — Glossary of Terms and Abbreviations

Alphabetical table of abbreviations and terms used across this manual. Expand from the course overview glossary and includes terms introduced in Parts 4 and 5. All voltage and frequency references follow the IEC basis of this manual (11 kV MV, 400/415 V LV, 50 Hz).

Term / Abbreviation Meaning and context in this manual
ACBAir Circuit Breaker. Large LV breaker used for switchboard incomers and the bus-tie on LV-MSB. Ratings typically 1600–6300 A; short-time withstand up to 65–100 kA (1 s).
AFBArc-Flash Boundary. The working distance at which incident energy falls to 1.2 cal/cm² (the onset-of-a-second-degree-burn threshold). Determined per IEEE 1584-2018.
ANSIAmerican National Standards Institute. Device numbering scheme (e.g. 50/51, 87T) used on SLD relay/breaker labels as industry convention alongside IEC.
ATSAutomatic Transfer Switch. Monitors the utility supply and, on failure, automatically transfers the essential bus to the standby DG. A failed-to-transfer ATS is one of the highest-RPN items in a typical FMECA.
Availability (A)A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). The fraction of calendar time a component or system is in its functioning state. Expressed as a decimal (e.g. 0.9992) or as annual downtime hours.
AVRAutomatic Voltage Regulator. Controls the excitation of the diesel generator alternator to maintain output voltage within limits during load changes.
BESSBattery Energy Storage System. Grid-connected or plant-connected battery system providing ride-through energy, inertia emulation, fast frequency / voltage support, or islanding capability.
BT (or B/T)Bus-Tie. The normally-open ACB (or contactor) connecting Bus A and Bus B of the LV-MSB split busbar. Closes automatically on loss of one incomer to allow the healthy transformer to back-feed the dead bus. A critical SPOF-mitigation element.
Cable ampacityThe continuous current-carrying capacity of a cable under defined installation conditions (temperature, grouping, burial). Determined from IEC 60364 / manufacturer tables after applying derating factors.
CT / VTCurrent Transformer / Voltage Transformer. Instrument transformers that step down MV/LV currents and voltages to safe measurement levels (e.g. 5 A / 110 V secondary). Feed protection relays, metering and SCADA.
DBDistribution Board. Final-circuit LV panel fed from the LV-MSB or an MCC; supplies lighting, small power and HVAC loads via MCBs/RCBOs.
DCDBDC Distribution Board (battery and charger system). Provides 110 V DC (typical) for MV switchgear trip/close coils and protection relay auxiliary supplies. A DCDB failure silences protection — very high severity in the FMECA.
DCSDistributed Control System. Plant-wide process control system with operator HMI. Receives electrical status (breaker position, voltages, currents) and issues high-level electrical commands.
DGDiesel Generator set. Standby power source sized for the essential-load bus (~800–1250 kVA indicative). Includes AVR, governor, fuel day-tank, battery starting and acoustic enclosure.
DOLDirect-On-Line. Simplest motor starting method — motor connected straight to supply. Starting current typically 5–7 × FLC; suitable for motors up to ~7.5 kW or where the bus can absorb the inrush.
DVRDynamic Voltage Restorer. Series power-electronics device injecting a compensating voltage to protect plant loads against upstream voltage sags and swells.
EDBEssential Distribution Board. Fed via the ATS from the DG (or utility essential bus). Supplies safety-critical loads that must remain energised during a utility outage.
FMECAFailure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis. Structured IEC 60812 method: list failure modes for each component, assess effects on the system, score Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detectability (D), compute RPN = S × O × D, and rank for action. The primary SPOF quantification tool in this campaign.
FLCFull-Load Current. The rated continuous current at full load: I = S / (√3 · V) for 3-phase. Basis for breaker sizing, cable selection and protection relay pickup settings.
GOOSEGeneric Object-Oriented Substation Event. IEC 61850 fast peer-to-peer message over the station bus used for inter-IED tripping, bus-transfer logic and load-shedding. Latency typically <4 ms.
IEDIntelligent Electronic Device. Numerical multifunction protection relay performing overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), differential (87T), voltage (27/59) and other protection functions, plus measurement and control.
IscShort-Circuit Current (symmetrical). The prospective fault current at a bus under bolted three-phase conditions. Used to specify the breaking capacity (Icu) of switchgear. Indicative: ~38.5 kA per 1600 kVA, Z = 6 % transformer.
LPLLightning Protection Level. Classes I–IV per IEC 62305, determined by risk assessment. Sets air-termination geometry, down-conductor spacing and earth-electrode requirements.
LPSLightning Protection System. The complete system of air terminations, down conductors and earth electrodes protecting the plant structure and equipment against direct lightning strikes.
LVLow Voltage. Utilisation voltage; in this manual 400/415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz (IEC: ≤ 1 kV AC). Confirm per plant.
LVRT / UVRTLow / Under Voltage Ride-Through. The capability or protection settings that allow plant equipment (particularly VFD-driven loads) to remain connected through a defined voltage dip rather than tripping and exacerbating the disturbance.
MCCMotor Control Centre. LV assembly of motor starter bays (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD) feeding process motors. Tags MCC-1 … MCC-n per the plant scheme.
MCCBMoulded-Case Circuit Breaker. Compact LV protective device for outgoing feeders and motor circuits. Breaking capacity ratings from ~10 kA to 85 kA; motor-protection versions incorporate thermal/magnetic or electronic trip units.
MMSManufacturing Message Specification. IEC 61850 protocol for client–server monitoring, control and data exchange between IEDs and SCADA/EMS. Complements GOOSE for non-time-critical data.
MTBFMean Time Between Failures. MTBF = 1/λ (h). Average operating time between successive failures. Higher MTBF = lower failure rate = more reliable. See Appendix G for indicative values.
MTTRMean Time To Repair / Restore. Average time from failure occurrence to return to service (detection + logistics + repair + recommissioning). MTTR is the key lever that automation (auto bus-tie, ATS) acts on — reducing it from hours (manual) to seconds (auto-transfer) dramatically raises availability.
MVMedium Voltage. Higher distribution voltage; in this manual 11 kV (IEC: 1 kV < MV ≤ 36 kV). Actual utility voltage must be confirmed per plant.
MV-METMV Utility Revenue Metering Cubicle. Contains utility-grade CTs/VTs and the tariff meter at the point of supply. Not a protection device — verify its presence and metering-point location during desk study.
MV-NERNeutral Earthing Resistor. Connected between the MV transformer neutral and earth to limit earth-fault current (typically 300–400 A) while keeping faults detectable. R ≈ 21 Ω for 300 A at 11 kV (indicative).
MV-SWGRMV Metal-Clad (or metal-enclosed) Switchgear. 11 kV bus with vacuum circuit breakers; incomer, bus-section and transformer-feeder panels. Rated for prospective fault level (indicative: 25 kA/1 s).
N+1 / N-1N+1: one more capacity unit than the minimum required — so one can be lost without losing the function. N-1: the condition after one unit has failed; the system must still serve the essential load. The 2 × 1600 kVA + bus-tie arrangement is N-1 secure for essential load (~55 % of total).
NERNeutral Earthing Resistor — see MV-NER.
PCCPower Control Centre. The main LV switchboard that distributes power to MCCs and outgoing feeders. In this manual, LV-MSB is the PCC. Distinct from an MCC (which only controls motors).
PFCPower Factor Correction. Automatic capacitor bank (with 7 % detuned reactors to handle VFD harmonics) that raises plant power factor from ~0.85 to ≥ 0.95, reducing reactive demand and transformer / cable loading.
PMSPower Management System. Dedicated electrical controller responsible for: load shedding on generation failure, automatic bus transfer (bus-tie / ATS), generator synchronisation, and energy management. Interfaces with the DCS.
PPEPersonal Protective Equipment. Arc-rated PPE is required for work on or near energised electrical equipment; the required category is set by the arc-flash study (incident energy in cal/cm²). Non-intrusive site surveys in this campaign do not require arc-rated PPE if live-panel access is not needed — but confirm with the host's QHSE procedure.
QHSEQuality, Health, Safety and Environment. Management system framework (ISO 9001 / 14001 / 45001) applied to all site activities. All surveys in this campaign are non-intrusive; QHSE and permit-to-work rules of the host facility always take precedence.
RAMReliability, Availability, Maintainability. The quantitative study of system uptime: how often failures occur (R), what fraction of time the system works (A), and how quickly it is restored (M). See Part 3 — FMECA / RAM.
RBDReliability Block Diagram. Series / parallel model used to compute system availability from component data. Series elements degrade availability; parallel (redundant) paths multiply it up. See Part 3.
ROCOFRate of Change of Frequency. Measure of frequency volatility on the grid (Hz/s). Increases as synchronous generation (with inertia) is displaced by inverter-based renewables. High ROCOF can trip plant protection (df/dt relays) prematurely.
RPNRisk Priority Number. FMECA criticality score = Severity (S) × Occurrence (O) × Detection (D), each scored 1–5 (higher = worse outcome / more frequent / harder to detect). RPN max = 125. Used to rank FMECA findings for action priority. Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. See Appendix C.
SCADASupervisory Control and Data Acquisition. Supervisory monitoring and control layer for the power system; aggregates IED data, trends energy, and provides alarm management to operators.
SLDSingle-Line Diagram. One-line schematic showing the power system's main components and their electrical connections, using IEC 60617 symbols. The primary document for SLD verification on site and for SPOF hunting.
SPOFSingle Point of Failure. A component or path whose failure alone disables a function or supply that must remain available. The central concept of this assessment campaign. See Part 3 and Appendix F.
STATCOM / SVCStatic Synchronous Compensator / Static VAr Compensator. Fast dynamic reactive-power devices used for voltage stabilisation and flicker / voltage-sag mitigation on weak or renewable-heavy grids.
TN-STN-S earthing system. IEC 60364 LV earthing arrangement where the neutral (N) and protective earth (PE) conductors are kept separate throughout. Used at 400 V LV in this plant.
TX-1 / TX-2MV/LV power transformers 1 and 2. Each indicatively 1600 kVA, 11 kV / 0.4 kV, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %, feeding the split LV-MSB busbar. Both together provide N-1 redundancy via the bus-tie.
UPSUninterruptible Power Supply. Battery-backed, no-break clean AC supply for the DCS/PLC/SCADA, field instrumentation and critical communications. Typical battery autonomy 15–30 min (to be specified per plant).
VFD / VSDVariable Frequency Drive / Variable Speed Drive. Power-electronic converter providing continuously variable speed control of AC motors (pumps, fans, compressors). Reduces starting current to near FLC; generates harmonics requiring detuned PFC.
ZSIZone-Selective Interlocking. Communication scheme between LV ACBs and MCCBs that allows the faulted zone's device to trip instantaneously while upstream devices remain on their normal graded delay — achieving both selectivity and fast arc-flash clearance.
λ (lambda)Failure rate (failures per hour). λ = 1/MTBF. The primary input to reliability calculations. See Appendix G for indicative values per equipment class.
Key takeaway: 46 terms defined. Use this table when reading the manual in the field or when completing FMECA and report forms. Cross-references to the relevant manual sections are embedded in key definitions.

B — IEC / IEEE Standards Reference

The table below lists the principal standards cited across this manual and the reference training material. The "Where it applies" column states the specific use in an MV/LV process-plant SPOF assessment context. This manual is standalone — the descriptions below are sufficient context; you do not need to hold copies of these standards in the field, but you should be aware of their scope.

Standard Title / Scope Where it applies in this manual
IEC 60034 (series) Rotating electrical machines — general requirements and ratings for AC motors. Motor nameplate data verification; LV-vs-MV feed threshold (~150–200 kW); MCC starter selection.
IEC 60076 (series) Power transformers — ratings, losses, impedance, tap changers, test methods. Transformer rating and Z% verification (TX-1/TX-2); N-1 loading check; fault-level calculation input.
IEC 60364 (series) Low-voltage electrical installations — design, protection, cable selection, earthing (TN-S, TT, IT), derating rules. Cable sizing and derating (§6, Module 2); LV TN-S earthing; distribution board design. Applied to LV-MSB, MCC, DB/EDB.
IEC 60812 Analysis techniques for system reliability — FMEA / FMECA procedure, including criticality analysis and RPN scoring. The FMECA form (Appendix E, Form 2) follows IEC 60812 column structure. All FMECA worksheets in the campaign use this method. See Part 3 — FMECA.
IEC 60831 Shunt power capacitors for AC power systems — capacitor bank ratings, protection. PFC bank specification and protection (PFC tag); detuned reactor sizing for VFD harmonic environment.
IEC 60909 Short-circuit currents in three-phase AC systems — calculation method for prospective fault currents. Short-circuit study (§4, Module 2). Used with power-system software (ETAP / DIgSILENT) to size breaking capacity of LV-MSB, MCC and all ACB/MCCB.
IEC 60947-2 Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear — circuit breakers (ACB and MCCB ratings, breaking capacity Icu/Ics, trip-unit types). ACB / MCCB specification for LV-MSB incomers, bus-tie and outgoing feeders; breaking-capacity verification against fault level from IEC 60909 study.
IEC 61078 Reliability block diagrams — construction rules, series/parallel formulae, system availability computation. RBD analysis in Module 5 and Part 3; quantifying availability benefit of N+1 transformer + bus-tie redundancy.
IEC 61439-1/-2 Low-voltage switchgear assemblies — type-tested assemblies: Form segregation, busbar bracing, temperature rise, short-circuit withstand. Specification and verification of LV-MSB (Form 4b) and MCC assemblies. Check form segregation during site visit.
IEC 61800 (series) Adjustable speed electrical power drive systems — ratings, EMC, protection, safety. VFD specification; harmonic emission from VFD drives (input to PFC detuning requirement).
IEC 61850 Communication networks and systems for power utility automation — station bus, GOOSE (fast tripping/transfer), MMS (monitoring). Defines data models for IED interoperability. PMS integration; GOOSE-based fast bus-transfer and load shedding (Module 4). Present on digital substations; check whether IEC 61850 is deployed during desk study.
IEC 62271-100 High-voltage AC circuit breakers — ratings, making/breaking capacity, tests. MV circuit breaker specification (vacuum CBs in MV-SWGR).
IEC 62271-200 AC metal-enclosed switchgear and controlgear for rated voltages above 1 kV and up to and including 52 kV — construction, type testing, internal arc classification (IAC). Specification and condition assessment of MV-SWGR during site visit; IAC rating relevant to arc-flash risk at MV level.
IEC 62305 (series) Protection against lightning — risk assessment (Part 2), physical damage (Part 3), electrical and electronic systems (Part 4). Lightning protection assessment (§12, Module 2); LPS class selection; bonding to earth grid. Applies to all plant buildings and outdoor structures.
IEEE 80 Guide for safety in AC substation grounding — touch voltage, step voltage, earth grid design. Earth-grid design and touch/step voltage compliance (§12, Module 2); MV substation earthing verification.
IEEE 493 Recommended practice for the design of reliable industrial and commercial power systems ("Gold Book") — reliability data, design methods, redundancy principles, RBD analysis. Primary source of indicative equipment failure-rate and MTBF data (see Appendix G). Referenced in Module 5 (FMECA & RAM) and the FMECA form.
IEEE 1584 Guide for performing arc-flash hazard calculations — empirical model for incident energy and arc-flash boundary at AC equipment. Arc-flash / incident-energy study (§11, Module 2). Preferable (not required) in this campaign. Determines PPE category and arc-flash boundary at LV-MSB and MCC.
ISO 14224 Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries — collection and exchange of reliability and maintenance data for equipment. Defines equipment taxonomy, failure modes and data formats. Equipment taxonomy for populating the FMECA and Document Inventory (SF-01). Cross-referenced for failure-rate data alongside IEEE 493.
ISO 20815 Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries — production assurance and reliability technology. RAM / RBD availability analysis methodology (Module 5); preferable study deliverable in this campaign.
NFPA 70E Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace (USA/ANSI). Arc-flash PPE categories, boundary definitions, energised work permits. Arc-flash PPE selection and labelling (§11, Module 2); host QHSE requirements may reference NFPA 70E even in IEC-governed plants. Non-intrusive surveys should not trigger NFPA 70E energised-work requirements.
Note: Standard numbers and editions are indicative. Always confirm the governing edition with the project specification and local regulatory authority. For Saudi Arabia plants, NEC/Saudi Electricity Company (SEC) technical standards may also apply and take precedence over the IEC defaults listed above.
Key takeaway: 20 standards referenced. IEC 60812 (FMECA), IEC 60909 (fault level), IEC 62271-200 (MV switchgear) and IEEE 493 (reliability data) are the four you will use most in this campaign.

C — Formula Reference Card

Consolidated printable reference of every key electrical calculation formula used in this manual. For worked examples and derivations see Part 2 — Calculations. All quantities are SI; constants: √3 = 1.732, 1 pu = 100 %. All values are indicative.

Quantity Formula Symbols Indicative example (this plant)
Apparent power S = P / pf S: apparent power (kVA/MVA); P: active power (kW/MW); pf: power factor. 2.0 MW / 0.85 = 2.35 MVA
3-phase current (from S) I = S / (√3 × V) I: line current (A); S: apparent power (VA); V: line-to-line voltage (V). 1600 kVA @ 400 V → 2310 A (TX LV FLC)
Motor full-load current I_fl = P / (√3 × V × pf × η) P: shaft power (W); V: line voltage (V); pf: motor pf; η: efficiency. 110 kW, 400 V, pf 0.86, η 0.95 → 194 A
DOL starting current I_start ≈ 6–7 × I_fl Use 6.5 × as indicative; confirm with motor manufacturer's data. 6.5 × 194 A = 1260 A
Transformer loading (pu) Loading = S_load / S_rated Values > 1.0 pu indicate overload; N-1 loading must be ≤ 1.0 pu (continuous). 1.175 / 1.600 = 0.73 pu (normal); 1.29 / 1.60 = 0.81 pu (N-1)
Short-circuit current I_sc = I_fl / Z(pu) I_fl: transformer LV FLC (A); Z(pu): transformer impedance as decimal (e.g. 0.06). Assumes infinite upstream source — conservative. 2310 / 0.06 = 38.5 kA per TX
Cable derating — required rating I_z(req) = I_B / (k_g × k_t) I_B: design (load) current (A); k_g: grouping factor; k_t: temperature factor. Select cable with tabulated I_z ≥ I_z(req). 194 / (0.80 × 0.94) = 258 A → 95 mm² Cu XLPE
Voltage drop (3-phase feeder) ΔV = √3 × I × (R·cosφ + X·sinφ) × L I: current (A); R, X: conductor resistance / reactance (Ω/km); L: length (km); cosφ/sinφ from load pf. 194 A, 120 m, 95 mm² Cu → 9.6 V = 2.4 % (limit ≤ 5 %)
PFC reactive power required Q_c = P × (tanφ1 − tanφ2) P: active power (kW); φ1: present pf angle; φ2: target pf angle. 2000 × (0.620 − 0.329) = 582 kVAr → ~600 kVAr bank
Generator sizing (essential load) S_DG ≥ (P_ess / pf) × k_step P_ess: essential active power (kW); pf: generator operating pf (0.8); k_step: step-load factor (~1.25). (0.65 MW / 0.8) × 1.25 = 1.01 MVA → ~1000 kVA DG
Transformer voltage regulation ΔV% ≈ load_pu × (R%·cosφ + X%·sinφ) load_pu: per-unit loading; R%, X%: transformer resistance and reactance in percent. 0.73 × (1.2×0.9 + 5.9×0.436) = 2.7 %
Protection grading margin t_up = t_down + Δt Δt: grading margin (typically 0.3 s) accounting for CB opening time, relay overshoot, CT error and safety margin. LV incomer 0.20 s → MV feeder relay 0.50 s
Arc-flash boundary AFB ≈ D × √(E / 1.2) D: working distance (m); E: incident energy at D (cal/cm²); 1.2 cal/cm² = onset-of-burn threshold. Simplified inverse-square scaling (full IEEE 1584 model required for labelling). 0.6 m × √(10/1.2) = 1.7 m
NER resistance R_NER = (V_LL / √3) / I_ef V_LL: MV line-to-line voltage (V); I_ef: target earth-fault current (A). Rate the NER for the fault duration (e.g. 10 s). (11,000 / 1.732) / 300 ≈ 21 Ω
Earth conductor (adiabatic) A = I × √t / k A: conductor cross-section (mm²); I: fault current (A); t: clearing time (s); k: material constant (143 for Cu/PVC). 38,500 × √0.2 / 143 = 120 mm² Cu
Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) MTBF: mean time between failures (h); MTTR: mean time to repair (h). Annual downtime = (1 − A) × 8760 h. TX: 200,000 / (200,000 + 168) = 0.99916 → ~7.4 h/yr
Series availability (RBD) A_sys = A1 × A2 × … × An All elements must function; system availability is always lower than the worst individual element. 0.9992 × 0.9994 = 0.9986 (e.g. incomer + TX in series)
Parallel availability 1-of-2 (RBD) A_par = 1 − (1 − A1)(1 − A2) Function survives if at least one element works. Much higher than series for the same components. 1 − (1−0.99916)² = 0.99999929 → ~22 s/yr down
FMECA Risk Priority Number RPN = S × O × D S: Severity (1–5); O: Occurrence (1–5); D: Detection difficulty (1–5; 5 = hardest to detect). RPN max = 125. Higher RPN = higher risk. Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. Rank and act top-down. ATS: S=5, O=3, D=4 → RPN = 60 (P1 in example FMECA)
Reliability (survival function) R(t) = e(−λt) λ: failure rate (failures/h) = 1/MTBF; t: mission time (h). R(t) = probability no failure in time t. λ = 5×10-6/h, t = 8760 h → R = e−0.044 = 0.957
Tip: For arc-flash and short-circuit studies, always use recognised power-system software (ETAP, DIgSILENT, SKM or equivalent) with the full IEC 60909 / IEEE 1584 models. The simplified formulas above are for quick manual checks and teaching purposes only.
Key takeaway: 20 formulas on one page. The three you will use most in the field are the FLC formula, the short-circuit formula (I_sc = I_fl / Z), and the availability formula (A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)). For full calculations, use the Part 2 worked examples.

D — Tag Scheme and SLD Conventions

Full tag table and IEC 60617 symbol key used across this manual, the training modules, and the master single-line diagram. Use only these tags when completing assessment forms. Source: the reference design document and Part 1 — Equipment Function Table.

Full Equipment Tag Table

Tag Equipment Voltage level Function in this plant
MV-MET Utility revenue metering cubicle 11 kV (MV) Utility billing / revenue metering point. Contains utility-grade CTs and VTs and the tariff meter. Not a protection or switching device.
MV-SWGR MV metal-clad switchgear 11 kV (MV) The main 11 kV distribution assembly. Houses the utility incomer CB (52-I), MV bus-section CB, transformer-feeder CBs (52-T1, 52-T2) with protection IEDs (50/51, 50N/51N, 87T). Rated ~25 kA/1 s. Fault level basis for all downstream short-circuit ratings.
MV-PROT MV protection and control IEDs 11 kV (MV) Numerical IEDs providing overcurrent (50/51), earth-fault (50N/51N), transformer differential (87T), and under/over-voltage (27/59) protection. Relays are supplied from the DCDB so they remain operative even on AC failure. A latent FMECA row — loss of DC supply to MV-PROT disables protection.
MV-NER Neutral Earthing Resistor 11 kV (MV) Resistance-earthing of the MV system neutral. Limits earth-fault current to ~300–400 A (indicative), controlling arc damage while keeping faults detectable. R ≈ 21 Ω (indicative at 11 kV, 300 A). Rated for fault duration (e.g. 10 s).
TX-1 MV/LV power transformer 1 11 kV / 400 V boundary Steps down 11 kV to 400 V for LV distribution. Indicative: 1600 kVA, Dyn11, Z ≈ 6 %, ONAN or cast-resin. Feeds Bus A of LV-MSB. Normal loading ~73 % (indicative).
TX-2 MV/LV power transformer 2 11 kV / 400 V boundary As TX-1. Feeds Bus B of LV-MSB. Together with TX-1, the two transformers provide N-1 redundancy via the bus-tie (BT).
LV-MSB Main LV switchboard / PCC 400 V (LV) The Power Control Centre. Split busbar (Bus A / Bus B) with ACB incomers from TX-1 and TX-2, a motorised normally-open bus-tie (BT), and MCCB outgoing feeders to MCCs, VFDs, PFC, UPS and DBs. Form 4b segregation (indicative). Bus rated ~2500–3200 A, 50–65 kA withstand.
BT Bus-tie circuit breaker 400 V (LV) The normally-open ACB (or contactor) connecting Bus A and Bus B of LV-MSB. Closes automatically (via PMS/ATS logic) on loss of one LV incomer to allow the surviving transformer to back-feed the dead bus. The bus-tie is a critical SPOF-mitigation element; verify its auto-transfer scheme is commissioned and periodically tested.
MCC-1 … MCC-n Motor Control Centres 400 V (LV) LV assemblies of motor starter bays (DOL, star-delta, soft-starter, VFD). Compartmentalised to IEC 61439; withdrawable or fixed. Each MCC feeds a group of process motors. For SPOF assessment: check whether duty and standby motors share the same MCC (common element — see Appendix F).
VFD Variable Frequency Drive(s) 400 V (LV) Power-electronic speed controllers for pumps, fans and compressors. Injects harmonics into the supply — requiring detuned PFC capacitors. Also provides LVRT capability and kinetic buffering through voltage sags.
PFC Power factor correction bank 400 V (LV) Automatic capacitor bank with 7 % detuned reactors. Corrects plant pf from ~0.85 to ~0.95+. Sized ~580–600 kVAr (indicative). Detuning prevents resonance with VFD harmonic currents. Loss of PFC causes reactive demand increase and potential utility penalty.
DG Diesel generator set 400 V (LV) Standby power source for the essential bus. Indicative rating ~800–1250 kVA; includes AVR, governor, day-tank, battery starting. Connected via the ATS. Verify weekly auto-test-on-load schedule, fuel level and battery condition during site visit.
ATS Automatic Transfer Switch 400 V (LV) Detects loss of utility supply and automatically starts and connects the DG to the essential bus. A P1-priority item (RPN 60) in the example FMECA. A failed-to-transfer ATS on a utility outage = total loss of essential load. Verify commissioning records and test schedule.
UPS Uninterruptible Power Supply 400 V AC / battery No-break, clean AC supply for DCS/PLC/SCADA, field instrumentation and critical communications. Battery autonomy typically 15–30 min (to be specified per plant). Loss of UPS during a supply disturbance can cause a DCS/PLC crash — verify battery condition and bypass availability.
DCDB DC distribution board (battery and charger) 110 V DC Provides DC supply for MV switchgear trip / close coils and protection relay auxiliary circuits. A DCDB failure can silence all MV protection — extremely high severity. Verify battery condition (float voltage, impedance test) and charger operation.
DB Distribution board (normal) 400 V (LV) Final-circuit panel for lighting, small power, HVAC, and sockets. Fed from LV-MSB or MCC outgoing feeders. MCB/RCBO protection.
EDB Essential distribution board 400 V (LV) As DB, but supplied via the ATS from the essential bus (DG-backed). Feeds safety-critical loads that must remain live during a utility outage (e.g. emergency lighting, fire-alarm panel, safety system auxiliaries).

IEC 60617 Symbol Key

The master SLD and all SPOF example diagrams use IEC 60617 symbols rendered by the slddraw.py library. The table below summarises the symbols you will encounter when reading or red-lining the drawings on site.

Symbol description IEC 60617 representation (text description for field use) Used in this plant for
Utility / grid source Circle with AC sine-wave mark inside 11 kV utility feeder supply connection
Circuit breaker (closed) Solid filled square on conductor All normally-closed breakers: MV CBs, LV ACBs (incomers), MCCB feeders
Circuit breaker (open / N.O.) Hollow (outline) square on conductor Bus-tie (BT) — normally open; auto-closes on N-1
Isolator / disconnector Open knife-contact symbol on conductor Maintenance isolation points upstream of CBs
Fuse Rectangle on conductor MV HRC fuses (where used); LV MCB/fuse in final circuits
Two-winding transformer Two interlinked circles (Dyn11 — delta primary, star secondary with neutral) TX-1 and TX-2
Neutral earthing resistor Resistor symbol in series to earth on the transformer neutral conductor MV-NER (resistance earthing of the 11 kV neutral)
Earth / ground Three horizontal bars of diminishing length All earth connections; transformer neutral solidly earthed at 400 V (TN-S)
Capacitor bank (PFC) Two parallel plates (capacitor symbol) PFC detuned capacitor bank on LV-MSB Bus A or B
Motor Circle with letter M Process motors (pumps, fans, compressors) fed from MCCs
Generator Circle with letter G DG alternator symbol
Labelled equipment block Rectangle with tag name and sub-label MCC, VFD, UPS, ATS, DB, EDB, DCDB
Busbar Bold horizontal line MV bus (firebrick colour); LV Bus A / Bus B (navy colour) in the SLD
SPOF indicator (red-lined SLD) Red dashed ring around the failure-point element with red caption Used on red-line mark-ups to flag identified SPOFs. Not a standard IEC symbol.
Tip — reading the SLD on site: When verifying the drawing against the as-found plant, check: (1) bus-tie switch — is it normally open and does it have an auto-transfer scheme? (2) transformer taps — what position are they set at? (3) ATS — is the transfer scheme commissioned? (4) DG — is the auto-start enabled? These four items account for most SPOF findings on routine site visits.
Key takeaway: 17 equipment tags (including MV-PROT), 14 IEC 60617 symbols. The tag scheme is fixed — do not invent new tags; update the reference document if a new item is found on a plant that does not fit any existing tag.

E — Blank Assessment Forms

Printable blank versions of the four per-plant deliverable forms. Open all four as soon as a plant is assigned; fill the header during desk study so the form is ready on site. See Part 0 — The Four Deliverable Forms for descriptions of what each form contains and when it is completed. The filled-in example in Part 5 shows how a completed set looks.

SF-01 — Document Inventory

Record all electrical documents requested, received and reviewed. Add rows as required. Documents not received should be chased via the site contact before or during the site visit. Form IDs SF-01…SF-04 match the worked example in Part 5 — Jubail Case Study.

Plant name
Plant reference / ID
Assessment reference
Engineer (lead)
Date desk study started
Date last updated
Ref. Document title Drawing / document number Revision Date Source (client / site / engineer) Status (received / requested / missing) Verified on site (Y / N / N/A) Discrepancies / notes
DOC-01Master Single-Line Diagram
DOC-02MV Switchgear Single-Line Diagram
DOC-03LV Switchboard (LV-MSB) Single-Line Diagram
DOC-04MCC-1 Single-Line / Schematic
DOC-05MCC-2 Single-Line / Schematic (if present)
DOC-06Equipment schedule / load list
DOC-07Transformer test certificates (TX-1, TX-2)
DOC-08Protection relay settings (MV-SWGR IEDs)
DOC-09Protection coordination study
DOC-10Short-circuit study
DOC-11Load-flow study
DOC-12Earthing / lightning protection study
DOC-13Arc-flash study (if available)
DOC-14DG / ATS commissioning records
DOC-15UPS / DCDB battery maintenance records
DOC-16Previous SPOF / reliability assessment (if any)
(add rows as required)
Documents outstanding at site-visit date
Data calls issued (number)
Form status☐ Draft   ☐ For review   ☐ Final

SF-02 — FMECA Worksheet

Failure Mode, Effects and Criticality Analysis per IEC 60812. One row per item / failure mode. Score S, O, D on a 1–5 scale (5 = worst); RPN = S × O × D (max 125). Priority bands: P1 = SPOF with S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60; P2 = RPN 36–59; P3 = RPN 16–35; P4 < 16. See Part 3 — FMECA method for scale definitions. Scores are indicative; calibrate scales using IEEE 493 / ISO 14224 data for real studies.

Plant name
Assessment reference
Engineer
FMECA revision
Date
Item ref. Tag Function Failure mode Local effect System effect (worst case) Existing controls / detection S (1–5) O (1–5) D (1–5) RPN Recommended action Rec. ref.
FM-01
FM-02
FM-03
FM-04
FM-05
FM-06
FM-07
FM-08
FM-09
FM-10
(add rows as required — one row per item/failure-mode combination)
Total items assessed
P1 items (SPOF S ≥ 4 or RPN ≥ 60)
P2 items (RPN 36–59)
Form status☐ Draft   ☐ For review   ☐ Final

SF-03 — Recommendations Register

Ranked list of recommended modifications and improvements derived from the FMECA. One row per recommendation. Link each row back to the FMECA reference (Rec. ref. = FM-xx). Risk ratings: H = High, M = Medium, L = Low. Cost: H / M / L indicative only.

Plant name
Assessment reference
Engineer
Register revision
Date
Rec. ref. Finding / SPOF description Recommended modification Affected tag(s) FMECA ref. Risk before (H/M/L) Risk after (H/M/L) Cost (H/M/L) Priority (1/2/3) Status Notes
REC-01
REC-02
REC-03
REC-04
REC-05
REC-06
REC-07
REC-08
(add rows as required)
Total recommendations
Priority 1 (High risk, act now)
Priority 2 (Medium risk, plan)
Priority 3 (Low risk, monitor)
Form status☐ Draft   ☐ For review   ☐ Final

SF-04 — Site-Visit Report

Factual record of the site visit. Non-intrusive only — do not open energised panels, operate switches or perform live tests. Complete on the day of the visit; review with PM before issue.

Section A — Site and Visit Details

Plant name / location
Plant reference / ID
Assessment reference
Site visit date(s)
Lead engineer (assessor)
Accompanying engineer / technician
Plant contact / host representative
Host organisation
Permit-to-work reference(s)
Areas of plant accessed
Areas NOT accessed (reason)

Section B — Scope and Non-Intrusive Statement

Survey scope (tick all that apply) ☐ MV switchroom   ☐ LV switchroom   ☐ Transformer yard / enclosure
☐ MCC room   ☐ Generator room / enclosure   ☐ UPS / battery room
☐ Control room / DCS panels   ☐ Earthing / earth pits   ☐ Other: ___________
Non-intrusive confirmation ☐ Confirmed — no live panels opened, no switching operations performed, no live electrical testing. Visual inspection, nameplate reading and photographic documentation only.
Thermographic survey performed☐ Yes (host permit obtained)   ☐ No

Section C — Key Findings

Overall as-found condition (brief)
SLD discrepancies found
Equipment nameplate data confirming SLD (Y / N / partial)

ATS auto-transfer status☐ Auto enabled   ☐ Manual only   ☐ Not verified
DG auto-start status☐ Auto enabled   ☐ Manual only   ☐ Not verified

Visible housekeeping / condition concerns

Form status☐ Draft   ☐ For review   ☐ Final
Warning — NON-INTRUSIVE surveys only. Do not open energised panels, operate switches or breakers, or conduct any live electrical test. Visual inspection, nameplate reading and photography (where permitted) are the only permitted activities. Observe host permit-to-work, LOTO and QHSE rules at all times.
Form ID mapping — blank forms to worked example (Jubail, Part 5): SF-01 = Document Inventory (rows: DOC-xx); SF-02 = FMECA Worksheet (rows: FM-xx); SF-03 = Recommendations Register (rows: REC-xx); SF-04 = Site-Visit Report (observations: OBS-xx, photos: PH-xx, actions: ACT-xx). These IDs align with the Part 5 Jubail case study.
Key takeaway: Four blank forms — SF-01 Document Inventory, SF-02 FMECA Worksheet, SF-03 Recommendations Register, SF-04 Site-Visit Report. Open them at plant assignment, fill the header during desk study, and update iteratively through site visit, analysis and recommendations stages.

F — SPOF-Hardening Principles (One-Page Summary)

Distilled from Part 3 — SPOF Analysis and Module 3. Use this page as a rapid scan-reference when reviewing an SLD or writing recommendations. Every principle has a trade-off in cost, complexity and footprint — apply redundancy where the consequence of failure justifies it (critical / essential loads first). All values and configurations are indicative.

Principle What it means in practice Typical implementation (tags) SPOF it eliminates Cost / complexity
N+1 redundancy Provide at least one more capacity unit than the minimum. The surviving unit(s) must carry the essential load on loss of one unit. 2 × TX-1/TX-2 (each capable of carrying essential load at ~81 % loading on N-1); N+1 MCC feeders for critical motors. Single transformer / single source H capital; H benefit for critical loads
Bus segregation Split the LV busbar into independent sections (Bus A / Bus B) so a bus fault or maintenance on one section does not take the whole plant. LV-MSB split bus; Form 4b compartmentalisation of MCC assemblies. Single un-split LV bus (Diagram A) M capital; H benefit (contained fault zone)
Bus-tie with auto-transfer The normally-open bus-tie closes automatically within seconds on loss of one LV incomer, connecting the healthy transformer to the dead bus. N-1 loading must be verified (≤ 1.0 pu continuous). BT ACB with auto-transfer scheme via PMS or hard-wired interlock; loss-of-voltage / breaker-position logic. Periodic on-load test required. Two buses with no tie (Diagram B) M capital; very H benefit (MTTR reduced from hours to seconds)
Dual utility incomers Two independent utility feeds to the MV bus from different utility substations or circuits, with a closeable MV bus-section between them. Two incomer panels in MV-SWGR (52-I1, 52-I2); MV bus-section CB (52-BS). Single utility feeder / single MV incomer (Diagram C) H capital (utility connection); H benefit for essential-load continuity
Separate routing — duty vs standby Duty and standby motors / loads must be fed from different sources, MCCs and cable routes. A single cable, MCC bus or MCC incomer serving both duty and standby is a common-element SPOF. Duty pump on MCC-1 (Bus A), standby pump on MCC-2 (Bus B); cables in separate trays on opposite sides of plant. Shared cable / single MCC for duty + standby (Diagram D) M capital; H benefit (eliminates common-mode failure)
Standby generation (DG + ATS) A standby diesel generator, automatically started and connected via the ATS on utility loss, backs up the essential bus for safety-critical and process-critical loads. DG set (~1000 kVA indicative) + ATS + EDB. ATS transfer time typically < 10 s (depends on DG run-up time). Auto-test on load weekly; fuel and battery monitoring continuous. Total loss of supply to essential loads on utility outage H capital per kVA; H benefit for safety loads
UPS for control / protection No-break, clean AC supply for the DCS/PLC/SCADA and instrumentation. Prevents a supply disturbance from crashing the control system and causing a process trip or safety incident. UPS (battery autonomy 15–30 min indicative) + DCDB (110 V DC for MV switchgear trip / close coils). Loss of control / protection on any supply disturbance M capital; H benefit for control continuity and protection dependability
Condition monitoring and testing Scheduled on-load testing (DG / ATS weekly; bus-tie transfer periodically) and continuous condition monitoring (transformer oil / temperature, battery impedance, UPS health) reduces Detection score (D) in the FMECA without changing failure rate. Monitored via PMS / DCS; oil/temperature sensors on TX-1/TX-2; battery monitoring on UPS and DCDB. Latent failures in standby equipment (DG fails to start; bus-tie fails to close) L–M capital; H benefit (lowers RPN through better detection)
Fault containment and selectivity Graded protection (time-current coordination) ensures the device closest to a fault trips first, isolating the smallest possible zone. Zone-selective interlocking (ZSI) maintains both selectivity and fast arc-flash clearance. Relay / trip-unit settings in MV-SWGR IEDs and LV-MSB ACBs; ZSI between ACB and outgoing MCCBs. Upstream breaker trips on a downstream fault (wider zone lost than necessary) L capital (settings only, unless extra relays); H benefit for zone containment
Earthing integrity A continuous, low-impedance earth system (grid + bonding + NER) ensures earth faults are reliably detected, current limited, and touch/step voltages kept safe. MV-NER (R ≈ 21 Ω indicative); LV TN-S; earth grid (≤ 1 Ω indicative); 120 mm² Cu earth conductors (indicative); bonding to LV-MSB, MCC, cable armour. Undetected earth faults; personnel electrocution; equipment damage from high fault energy M capital; safety-critical

SPOF-Hunt Checklist — Quick Scan of an SLD

  • Is there a single utility incomer / single MV bus? (Diagram C SPOF — check for dual incomers or DG/ATS coverage).
  • Is there only one transformer? (Diagram A SPOF — check for N+1 or bus-tie capability).
  • Is the LV bus split into sections A and B? If not, the whole LV plant is a single zone.
  • Is there a bus-tie (BT) between Bus A and Bus B? Is it normally open with auto-transfer?
  • Are duty and standby motors fed from different MCCs on different buses, via separately routed cables? (Diagram D SPOF).
  • Is the DG connected via an ATS? Is the ATS auto-transfer commissioned and tested regularly?
  • Is there a UPS for DCS/PLC/SCADA? Is there a DCDB for MV switchgear trip/close supplies?
  • Are transformer differential protection (87T) and earth-fault protection (50N/51N) commissioned on both TX-1 and TX-2?
  • Is the NER present and rated correctly? (Check neutral CT and earth-fault relay are connected).
  • Is protection coordination current — does the study reflect the as-found breaker / relay configuration?
Key takeaway: Ten hardening principles; ten SPOF-hunt checklist items. The five most common findings are: no bus-tie auto-transfer, duty and standby on the same MCC, single utility incomer with no DG coverage, ATS not commissioned, and outdated protection coordination study. Address these first.

G — Indicative Failure-Rate and MTBF Data

Indicative λ (failure rate), MTBF and MTTR figures per equipment class, drawn from the reliability analysis in Module 5 and typical industrial power-system data. Use these values for preliminary FMECA scoring and RBD availability estimates only.

Warning — indicative values only. The figures in this table are indicative and tentative. They are representative of industrial power-system equipment in good condition under reasonable maintenance regimes. Actual failure rates vary significantly with age, maintenance history, environment (tropical, desert, coastal), load factor, manufacturer and installation quality. For any real reliability or availability study, these figures must be replaced with values confirmed against IEEE 493 (Gold Book), ISO 14224, or plant-specific historical maintenance data. Do not cite this table as a primary source in a formal study.
Equipment class Tag(s) Indicative λ (failures/yr) Indicative MTBF (h) Indicative MTTR (h) Indicative Availability A Notes
MV metal-clad switchgear (panel) MV-SWGR ~0.006 / yr ~150,000 h 168 h ~0.99889 Per panel (bus section, incomer or TX feeder). Includes vacuum CB and protection relay. MTTR includes spares logistics.
MV/LV power transformer TX-1, TX-2 ~0.004 / yr ~200,000 h 168–720 h ~0.99916 (at 168 h MTTR) Cast-resin units have lower fire risk but similar electrical failure rate to oil-filled. MTTR can extend to 720+ h if rewinding required. Oil/temp monitoring reduces latent failure.
LV switchboard / PCC (complete assembly) LV-MSB ~0.010 / yr ~90,000 h 8–72 h ~0.99920 (at 72 h MTTR) Busbar fault rare but high severity; most failures are in individual breakers or auxiliaries. Form 4b segregation limits fault zone and reduces effective MTTR.
Air Circuit Breaker (ACB) LV-MSB incomer / BT ~0.015 / yr ~60,000 h 4–24 h ~0.99960 (at 24 h MTTR) Includes failure-to-trip (most critical mode) and failure-to-close (bus-tie BT failure on auto-transfer). Regular trip testing improves Detection (D) score.
Moulded-Case Circuit Breaker (MCCB) Feeders in LV-MSB, MCC ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 2–8 h ~0.99982 (at 8 h MTTR) Higher failure rate reflects larger population; most failures affect only the individual feeder. Selectivity (coordination) prevents upstream trip.
Motor Control Centre (MCC, complete) MCC-1 … MCC-n ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 4–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Aggregate figure for the assembly including contactors, overloads and starters. A single starter failure affects only one motor; a bus fault or incomer failure affects all motors on that MCC.
Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) VFD ~0.050 / yr ~18,000 h 4–48 h ~0.99733 (at 48 h MTTR) Higher failure rate than simple contactors; power electronics are sensitive to temperature, harmonics and humidity. MTTR can be long if a power module must be ordered. Consider bypass contactor for critical drives.
Power factor correction bank (PFC) PFC ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 8–48 h ~0.99893 (at 48 h MTTR) Capacitor element failure is the dominant mode; detuned reactors are generally more reliable. Failure increases reactive demand but does not typically cause a supply outage — Medium severity.
Diesel generator set (complete) DG ~0.060 / yr (start-to-run) ~15,000 h (run) 4–48 h Conditional: P(start success) ~0.95 per demand Distinguish between failure-to-start (~5 % demand rate indicative) and failure-while-running (< 1 %/h). Weekly tested-on-load reduces P(fail to start). Fuel, battery and coolant condition are the main latent risks.
Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS) ATS ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 2–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Failure-to-transfer on demand is the critical mode (highest RPN in typical FMECA). Redundant control logic, position monitoring and monthly on-load tests materially reduce the Occurrence and Detection scores.
UPS (online, double-conversion) UPS ~0.030 / yr ~30,000 h 2–24 h ~0.99920 (at 24 h MTTR) Battery ageing is the dominant long-term failure mode; inverter electronics also fail. Battery impedance testing (annually) identifies degraded cells before failure. A bypass path and redundant UPS greatly improve system availability.
DC battery and charger (DCDB) DCDB ~0.020 / yr ~45,000 h 4–48 h ~0.99893 (at 48 h MTTR) Battery failure can silence all MV protection — extremely high severity. Float-voltage monitoring and annual discharge testing are essential. Keep a spare battery module on site for critical plants.
Neutral Earthing Resistor (NER) MV-NER ~0.002 / yr ~500,000 h 8–24 h ~0.99998 Passive element; low failure rate. Open-circuit or short-circuit failure both change earth-fault protection behaviour. Verify resistance and neutral CT integrity during assessment.
MV/LV feeder cable (per km) Feeders between tagged equipment ~0.003 / yr / km (LV XLPE) ~300,000 h / km 8–72 h ~0.99976 (at 72 h MTTR) Buried LV cable; failure rate increases with age, installation damage and proximity to other services. A single cable shared by duty and standby loads is a common-element SPOF (Diagram D): use separately routed cables.

RBD Availability Examples

Configuration Formula Indicative result Annual downtime
Single transformer (series) A = 0.99916 0.99916 ~7.4 h/yr
Two transformers 1-of-2 (parallel) A = 1 − (1−0.99916)2 0.99999929 ~22 s/yr
Transformer + ACB in series A = 0.99916 × 0.99960 0.99876 ~10.9 h/yr
ATS (manual MTTR ~8 h vs auto ~0.003 h) Manual: A = 30000/(30000+8) = 0.99973
Auto: A = 30000/(30000+0.003) = 0.99999990		 Manual: 0.99973
Auto: ~1.000		 Manual: ~2.3 h/yr
Auto: ~90 s/yr
DG + ATS (both must work) A = A_DG × A_ATS = 0.9980 × 0.99973
A_DG derivation: DG failure-to-start probability ≈ 5% per demand (1 demand/yr); MTBF running ≈ 15,000 h; MTTR ≈ 24 h. Effective A_DG = 1 − P(fail-to-start) ≈ 0.9980 (indicative — dominated by start-reliability; full RAM model needed for real studies).		 ~0.99773 ~19.6 h/yr (manual ATS)
Note — MTTR as the key lever: The ATS example illustrates why auto-transfer (MTTR reduced from 8 h to ~10 s) delivers a far larger availability improvement than any realistic reduction in failure rate. Auto-close bus-tie and auto-transfer ATS are the highest-value reliability upgrades in a typical MV/LV plant. This principle is central to the recommendations generated by this campaign.
Key takeaway: 14 equipment classes tabulated. Use these indicative figures for initial FMECA scoring only. Replace with IEEE 493 / ISO 14224 / plant maintenance records for formal studies. The single most impactful design choice visible in the MTBF data is the auto-transfer MTTR reduction: from ~8 h (manual) to seconds (auto), turning ~2 h/yr loss into < 2 min/yr.
